Each connection, whether harmonious or contentious, played a role in shaping the man who transformed technology, business, and culture. His story is not just one of individual genius, but of the profound impact of human connection on creativity and achievement.

Geoffrey Everest Hinton, born in 1947 in Wimbledon, England, is a British-Canadian scientist whose pioneering work in artificial neural networks has earned him the moniker “the Godfather of AI.” Hinton’s journey through the world of artificial intelligence is not just a story of individual brilliance, but one deeply interwoven with the contributions, mentorships, and collaborations of a remarkable network of scientists, students, and institutions.

Hinton’s intellectual lineage is notable: he is the great-great-grandson of George Boole, whose work underpins modern computer science, and Mary Everest Boole, a renowned educator. His father, Howard Hinton, was an entomologist, and his family tree includes other distinguished academics. This heritage of inquiry and innovation set the stage for Hinton’s own academic pursuits.

After attending Clifton College in Bristol, Hinton entered King’s College, Cambridge, where he explored various disciplines before settling on experimental psychology. He later earned his PhD in artificial intelligence at the University of Edinburgh under the supervision of Christopher Longuet-Higgins, a proponent of symbolic AI. This early exposure to differing schools of thought in AI would later inform Hinton’s own approach, which often challenged prevailing paradigms.

Hinton’s career is marked by his ability to foster and collaborate with a vibrant community of researchers. After initial academic appointments in the UK, Hinton moved to the United States, working at the University of California, San Diego, and Carnegie Mellon University. He later became the founding director of the Gatsby Computational Neuroscience Unit at University College London, before settling in Canada at the University of Toronto in 1987.

It was in Toronto that Hinton’s influence blossomed. He became a Fellow at the Canadian Institute for Advanced Research (CIFAR), where he helped launch and lead the “Neural Computation and Adaptive Perception” program. This initiative brought together a cohort of future luminaries, including Yoshua Bengio and Yann LeCun. The trio’s collaboration and friendly rivalry would drive the field of deep learning forward, culminating in their joint receipt of the 2018 Turing Award, often called the “Nobel Prize of Computing.” Their ongoing partnership, as well as their public discussions and debates, have shaped the direction of AI research globally.

Hinton’s impact is perhaps most evident in the success of his students and postdoctoral researchers. He mentored a generation of AI leaders, including Ilya Sutskever (co-founder of OpenAI), Alex Krizhevsky (co-creator of AlexNet), Yann LeCun (Chief AI Scientist at Meta), Ruslan Salakhutdinov, Zoubin Ghahramani, and many others. These protégés have gone on to lead major research labs, found influential companies, and make their own groundbreaking contributions.

The story of AlexNet, which revolutionized computer vision by winning the 2012 ImageNet challenge, is emblematic of Hinton’s collaborative spirit. Developed by Krizhevsky and Sutskever under Hinton’s guidance, AlexNet’s success demonstrated the power of deep neural networks and catalyzed a wave of investment and interest in AI. This achievement was not just a technical milestone, but a testament to the synergy between mentor and students.

Hinton’s research has often been collaborative. His 1986 paper with David Rumelhart and Ronald J. Williams popularized the backpropagation algorithm, a foundational technique for training neural networks. While not the first to propose the method, their work made it accessible and influential, sparking a resurgence of interest in neural networks.

In 1985, Hinton co-invented the Boltzmann machine with David Ackley and Terry Sejnowski, further expanding the toolkit for machine learning. His partnership with Laurens van der Maaten led to the development of t-SNE, a widely used data visualization technique. Hinton’s openness to collaboration and his willingness to credit others—such as acknowledging Rumelhart’s role in backpropagation—reflect his commitment to advancing the field collectively.

Beyond academia, Hinton has played a pivotal role in shaping the AI landscape through institutional leadership. In 2012, he co-founded DNNresearch Inc. with Krizhevsky and Sutskever, which was soon acquired by Google. This move allowed Hinton to split his time between Google Brain and the University of Toronto, bridging the gap between academic research and industrial application.

Hinton also co-founded the Vector Institute in Toronto, serving as its chief scientific advisor. These institutions have become hubs for AI research, attracting talent and fostering collaboration across disciplines and sectors.

Hinton’s achievements have been recognized with numerous awards, many of which he shares with his collaborators. In addition to the Turing Award with Bengio and LeCun, he received the 2024 Nobel Prize in Physics alongside John Hopfield for foundational work in neural networks. He has also been honored with the VinFuture Prize and the Queen Elizabeth Prize for Engineering, both awarded jointly with other leaders in the field.

These shared accolades underscore the collective nature of progress in AI, with Hinton often at the center of a web of innovators pushing the boundaries of what machines can learn.

In recent years, Hinton has become a prominent voice on the risks and ethical challenges of AI. His decision to leave Google in 2023 was motivated by a desire to speak freely about the dangers of unchecked AI development. He has called for international cooperation and regulation, warning of potential misuse, economic disruption, and existential threats.

Hinton’s views sometimes diverge from those of his peers. For example, while he has expressed deep concern about the possibility of AI surpassing human intelligence and the risks of catastrophic misuse, Yann LeCun has taken a more optimistic stance, believing AI could ultimately benefit humanity. These debates highlight the diversity of thought within the AI community, even among its leading figures.

Hinton’s personal life has been marked by both tragedy and resilience. He lost his first wife, Rosalind Zalin, and his second wife, Jacqueline Ford, to cancer. Despite chronic back pain and lifelong struggles with depression, Hinton has remained a passionate advocate for scientific inquiry and social responsibility. His political views, shaped in part by disillusionment with US policies, led him to settle in Canada, where he has contributed to building a vibrant AI ecosystem.

Geoffrey Hinton’s legacy is inseparable from the network of relationships he has cultivated throughout his career. As a mentor, collaborator, and leader, he has inspired and empowered others to push the frontiers of artificial intelligence. The story of AI’s rise is, in many ways, the story of Hinton and his extended intellectual family—a testament to the power of shared vision, rigorous debate, and collective achievement.

Lawrence Edward Page, born in 1973 in Lansing, Michigan, is renowned as the co-founder of Google, a company that has fundamentally transformed the way the world accesses information. Yet, the story of Larry Page is not just one of individual brilliance, but of deep and dynamic relationships—with family, friends, colleagues, and rivals—that shaped his journey and the evolution of Google.

Larry’s upbringing was steeped in technology and academia. His father, Carl Victor Page Sr., was a pioneering computer science professor, while his mother, Gloria, taught computer programming. The intellectual environment at home, filled with computers and science magazines, fostered Larry’s curiosity and creativity. Even after his parents’ divorce, Larry maintained close ties with both his mother and his father’s partner, Joyce Wildenthal, herself a professor. His older brother, Carl Jr., played a formative role, encouraging Larry’s habit of dismantling gadgets to understand their workings—a trait that foreshadowed his future as an inventor and entrepreneur.

Music also played a significant part in Larry’s development. His parents sent him to music camps, and he credits his musical training for instilling in him a sense of timing and a drive for speed—qualities that later influenced Google’s focus on rapid search results.

Larry’s academic path took him from Montessori school to the University of Michigan, where he engaged in inventive projects and leadership roles, and then to Stanford University for his PhD. At Stanford, Larry’s quest for a dissertation topic led him to explore the structure of the World Wide Web as a vast network of links. His advisor, Terry Winograd, encouraged this direction, providing crucial mentorship.

It was at Stanford that Larry met Sergey Brin, a fellow PhD student. Their partnership was both intellectual and personal, marked by spirited debates and a shared vision. Together, they developed the BackRub project, which evolved into the PageRank algorithm—the foundation of Google’s search technology. Their collaboration was not just technical; it was a meeting of minds that thrived on challenge and mutual respect. The duo’s synergy was so strong that their research paper became one of the most downloaded scientific documents of its time.

Other key contributors joined their early efforts, including Scott Hassan and Alan Steremberg, who provided technical expertise. The project’s rapid growth strained Stanford’s resources, but the team’s collective ingenuity kept it moving forward.

The transition from academic project to global enterprise was enabled by a network of supporters. Larry and Sergey raised funds from faculty, friends, and family, and famously received a $100,000 check from Sun Microsystems co-founder Andy Bechtolsheim before Google was even incorporated. Susan Wojcicki, a friend, rented them her garage, which became Google’s first office—a testament to the importance of personal relationships in the company’s early days.

As Google grew, Larry and Sergey’s complementary skills shaped the company’s culture and direction. Larry, as CEO, emphasized technical excellence and direct communication, sometimes clashing with traditional management structures. His belief in empowering engineers and minimizing bureaucracy reflected his trust in the abilities of those around him.

However, as Google attracted major investment, Larry was persuaded by investors and industry leaders—including Steve Jobs and Andy Grove—to step aside as CEO in favor of Eric Schmidt, who brought seasoned leadership. This decision, though difficult, demonstrated Larry’s willingness to adapt for the greater good of the company and his respect for the advice of experienced peers. Even after stepping down, Larry remained deeply involved, approving key hires and strategic decisions.

Larry’s vision extended beyond search. He led the acquisition of Android, working closely with Andy Rubin to make mobile computing ubiquitous. This move, initially made without Schmidt’s knowledge, underscored Larry’s entrepreneurial boldness and his trust in his collaborators.

Competition also shaped Larry’s journey. The rise of Facebook prompted Google to launch its own social network, Google+, led by Vic Gundotra. The rivalry with companies like Apple and Microsoft influenced major decisions, such as the acquisition of Motorola Mobility to protect Android’s patent portfolio.

Within Google, Larry fostered a culture of collaboration and innovation. Upon returning as CEO in 2011, he formed the “L-Team” of senior vice-presidents to enhance unity and communication. He also championed ambitious projects—“moonshots”—and encouraged teams to pursue ideas that were ten times better than existing solutions, a philosophy echoed by Astro Teller, head of Google X.

Larry’s leadership style evolved over time. Early on, he was known for intense debates, especially with Sergey, but later he prioritized harmony and teamwork, recognizing that ambitious goals required collective effort. He also mentored rising leaders like Sundar Pichai, who eventually succeeded him as CEO of Google and later Alphabet.

Larry’s interests extended into health and technology, leading to the creation of Calico, an initiative focused on longevity, with Art Levinson as CEO. He invested in ventures like Tesla, collaborating with Elon Musk, and supported startups in renewable energy and aviation, such as Kitty Hawk and Opener.

Larry’s personal relationships continued to influence his life. He briefly dated Marissa Mayer, a future tech leader, and later married Lucinda Southworth, a research scientist. Their family life, including two children, has been largely private, though Larry’s philanthropic efforts—such as significant donations to health research and Ebola relief—reflect his commitment to societal well-being.

Larry’s circle of friends and collaborators includes notable figures like Richard Branson, on whose island he was married, and Peter Diamandis, whose ideas about abundance and technology Larry supports. He also helped establish Singularity University, fostering a community of thinkers focused on the future of humanity.

Though Larry stepped back from day-to-day management in 2019, his influence endures through the people and structures he helped build. Alphabet, Google’s parent company, allows for independent innovation across diverse fields, a testament to Larry’s belief in empowering others.

Larry Page’s story is one of interconnectedness—of family nurturing curiosity, of academic partnerships sparking innovation, of business alliances and rivalries driving progress, and of a leader who recognized that the greatest achievements are realized not alone, but through collaboration with others.

Elon Reeve Musk, born in Pretoria, South Africa, in 1971, is a figure whose life and career have been deeply shaped by his relationships with family, business partners, rivals, and political leaders. Musk’s story is not just one of individual ambition, but of complex interactions with those around him—family members who influenced his values, collaborators who helped build his companies, and adversaries who shaped his public persona.

Musk’s early years were marked by the influence of his parents and extended family. His mother, Maye Musk, a Canadian-born model and dietitian, and his father, Errol Musk, an engineer and entrepreneur, provided a privileged upbringing. However, the family dynamic was complicated. After his parents’ divorce, Elon chose to live with his father, a decision he later regretted due to their strained relationship. Errol’s own controversial past, including his business dealings and political views, cast a long shadow over Elon’s formative years. Stories from his maternal grandfather, Joshua Haldeman—a political activist with radical views—fueled Elon’s sense of adventure and skepticism toward authority.

Elon’s siblings, Kimbal and Tosca, also played significant roles in his life. Kimbal would later become a business partner, while Tosca pursued a career in film. The Musk family’s wealth and connections provided Elon with opportunities, but also set high expectations and exposed him to both support and criticism from those closest to him.

Musk’s journey from South Africa to North America was facilitated by his mother’s Canadian citizenship, allowing him to avoid South Africa’s apartheid-era military service. In Canada, he relied on extended family for support, working odd jobs before enrolling at Queen’s University and later transferring to the University of Pennsylvania. During his studies, Musk formed important relationships, hosting parties to fund his education and collaborating on early business ideas.

His first major venture, Zip2, was co-founded with his brother Kimbal and Greg Kouri, with financial backing from their father. The trio’s partnership was crucial in navigating the challenges of a startup, from coding late into the night to negotiating with major newspapers. However, tensions with the company’s board led to Musk being sidelined from leadership, an early lesson in the complexities of business relationships.

Musk’s next venture, X.com, brought him into contact with a new set of collaborators and competitors. The merger with Confinity, led by Max Levchin and Peter Thiel, created PayPal. Here, Musk’s leadership style clashed with others, particularly Thiel, leading to power struggles and ultimately Musk’s ouster as CEO. Despite these conflicts, the relationships forged during this period—often referred to as the “PayPal Mafia”—would have lasting impacts, as many of these individuals went on to shape Silicon Valley.

With the proceeds from PayPal’s sale, Musk founded SpaceX, where he assembled a team of engineers and visionaries. His leadership was both inspiring and demanding, pushing colleagues to achieve breakthroughs in reusable rockets. Relationships with figures like NASA administrator Mike Griffin were instrumental in securing contracts that kept SpaceX afloat during difficult times. Musk’s willingness to risk his fortune and reputation inspired loyalty, but also led to high-pressure environments and occasional fallout with partners.

At Tesla, Musk’s role evolved from investor to CEO, following conflicts with founders Martin Eberhard and Marc Tarpenning. The ousting of Eberhard and subsequent legal battles highlighted Musk’s determination to assert control, but also the challenges of balancing vision with collaboration. Tesla’s success was built on the efforts of many, but Musk’s relationships with co-founders, engineers, and investors were often fraught with tension.

Musk’s family continued to play a role in his ventures. He provided the initial concept and funding for SolarCity, founded by his cousins Lyndon and Peter Rive. The acquisition of SolarCity by Tesla, however, led to lawsuits and accusations of self-dealing, straining relationships with shareholders and raising questions about the intersection of family and business.

Musk’s later ventures, including Neuralink and The Boring Company, brought together new teams of scientists, engineers, and business leaders. At Neuralink, Musk’s ambitious goals for brain-computer interfaces created both excitement and controversy, with employees and animal rights groups clashing over the pace and ethics of development. The Boring Company’s projects required navigating relationships with city officials and regulators, as well as managing public expectations.

His involvement with OpenAI and later xAI reflected shifting alliances in the world of artificial intelligence. Musk’s departure from OpenAI, due to disagreements over its direction, led him to recruit talent from competitors and leverage relationships with investors from his other companies.

Musk’s relationships with political figures have been particularly influential—and contentious. His acquisition of Twitter (renamed X) brought him into conflict with former executives, employees, and users, as he implemented sweeping changes and reduced content moderation. The platform’s role in spreading misinformation and hate speech drew criticism from governments and advocacy groups, further polarizing public opinion.

Musk’s political activities intensified with his support for far-right causes and his advisory role in the Trump administration’s Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE). His interactions with Donald Trump were both collaborative and adversarial, culminating in a public feud after Musk’s departure from government service. These relationships shaped not only Musk’s public image but also the broader political landscape, as his influence extended to campaign donations, policy decisions, and international controversies.

Throughout his career, Musk’s relationships—with family, business partners, rivals, and political leaders—have been central to his successes and failures. His ability to inspire, challenge, and sometimes alienate those around him has defined his leadership style. Whether collaborating with engineers to land rockets, clashing with co-founders over company direction, or sparring with world leaders on social media, Musk’s story is one of constant interaction and negotiation with others.

As of 2025, Musk remains a figure whose fortunes and failures are deeply intertwined with the people and institutions he engages with. His legacy will be shaped not only by his technological achievements but by the complex web of relationships that have propelled—and sometimes hindered—his extraordinary journey.

Character Introduction:
You are a highly talented surgeon. You have published numerous articles, your research spans the entire country, and you are renowned worldwide. You work in the emergency room because it is constantly challenging, requires intense concentration, and the salary is extremely high. In short, you are completely successful in your career, but not so much in your personal life.
You are religious, but you do not strictly adhere to your faith, so it seems that misfortune has always haunted your family. Five years ago, your wife died of dilated cardiomyopathy and heart failure. You took care of her meticulously but could only watch helplessly as she passed away. Until the very end, you hoped she could receive a heart transplant (the only way to save her). Unfortunately, both the long transplant waiting list and the difficulty of finding a suitable heart made the surgery hopeless. Even so, you kept practicing the techniques for performing a heart transplant for your wife. After her death, you had to learn to raise your son Scott alone.
You have noticed that your wife’s death has affected your work: you have become rougher and more unreasonable toward your subordinates and those colleagues you consider less capable. Additionally, you have reduced the number of surgeries you perform. Although you have become somewhat unstable, you are still one of the best surgeons in the country! As your reputation has slipped slightly, your colleagues believe you have fallen from genius to just a very good surgeon. Meanwhile, Hans Rimoret is becoming a rising star in the surgical field. He not only focuses on surgical theory, but also has solid technical skills and was born with hands perfectly suited for surgery. He often assists you in operations, and his extraordinary talent has left a deep impression on you. But then again, is he really destined to be an outstanding surgeon? Seven years ago, he was your intern. You were the mentor who taught him everything—this should be your achievement. However, your arrogance in believing you could remain at the top of the field for a long time has made you a bit jealous of Hans Rimoret; this is your flaw.
These days, medical journals constantly talk about Hans Rimoret’s talent, but the articles forget to mention your important influence on his career. Still, it doesn’t matter, because Hans Rimoret will never surpass your excellence.
After your wife’s death, your performance declined somewhat, but at the same time, your faith became stronger. Your belief helped you overcome your grief, slowly recover, and return to the peak of your skill.
Unfortunately, a second terrible event once again overturned your life. Last December, your only son Scott was the victim of a motorcycle accident and was quickly brought to the emergency room. The doctor on duty at that time was Hans Rimoret. As soon as you heard about the accident, you rushed to the hospital, but sadly, it was already too late... your son had died. Doctor Hans Rimoret did his best to save him and explained that Scott was already in critical condition when he arrived in the ER, and subsequent efforts were in vain. You read the medical report and even personally examined your son’s body. It was clear that Hans Rimoret was not at fault, but you simply could not accept this result. You even believe that if Hans Rimoret were as outstanding as you, he surely could have saved Scott. It’s hard for you to understand how the person touted as the next great American surgical "genius" after you could not save your poor son.
You haven’t told anyone that your world has collapsed. During that time, you began to drink heavily. At first, it was only at night, just to help you sleep, but later you started drinking during the day as well. Needless to say, the drinking has begun to affect your work. Your grief has drowned your professional progress. Although you try to keep up appearances, your attitude toward colleagues has become harsher, especially toward the nurses and doctors you consider increasingly unreliable. As for Hans Rimoret, you have hardly spoken to him...
When you were at your lowest, you often confided in Father Tom, who visits Brighton Hospital. During breaks between visiting two patients, he listened to your confession. You told him everything—both misfortunes, your drinking problem, and your growing resentment toward Hans Rimoret. Tom tried his best to comfort you, and under his advice, you participated in a "ten-step treatment program" guided by a psychiatrist. Recently, everything has been improving; your drinking problem has almost disappeared, and you have started to forgive and no longer blame Hans Rimoret for Scott’s death. Now, the only thing you truly value is your extraordinary surgical skill. You have set a goal for yourself: to be the best and remain the best. A new life is calling you.
Six months ago, Director Richard Berkeley assigned you a new intern, Erin Carter. So you started dealing with him, quickly making him your least favorite “project.” You gave him a mountain of dirty, tedious, and exhausting tasks, but never allowed him to observe or practice in the operating room. You find Erin Carter too mediocre and cannot stand working with such mediocrity. He is slow to understand your instructions, careless, and sometimes even daydreams. You feel he is completely unworthy of your mentorship, even though he has the chance to be an intern to the world’s greatest surgeon! Erin Carter is not worth your time at all! You plan to talk to Richard Berkeley about this...
Tonight
As usual, you have once again saved the lives of two or three patients, once again proving your dominance in the operating room. At the same time, you are once again unable to tolerate your colleagues’ and intern’s mediocrity. It’s certain that Erin Carter has demonstrated such incompetence that you can no longer endure him. You plan to take advantage of the director’s visit to the ER and try to have him assign Erin Carter to another doctor for training. Unfortunately, Hans Rimoret’s death and the hostage incident have ruined your plan.
Relationships:
Hans Rimoret (the deceased): You once blamed him for your son’s death, but recently you have tried to let it go. However, you rarely talked to him, mainly because you considered him a rival for the title of "the best surgeon." Nevertheless, you never had any intention of killing him.
Drake Rimoret: He is less talented than his brother Hans Rimoret, and thus less talented than you. However, he has a clear understanding of himself, which is why he balances medicine and laboratory research. You tend to think of him as an excellent pharmacologist rather than a distinguished surgeon.
Erin Carter: Your intern. His mediocrity irritates you, and his incompetence is difficult for you to comprehend every day. You try not to be too harsh, but it’s hard during surgical training. You hope he improves through hardship, but it seems he simply does not have what it takes to become a great doctor.
Director Richard Berkeley: You have little contact with him. This is the first time you’ve seen him in the ER in recent years. He forced Erin Carter onto you, proving that he’s an administrator, not a duty doctor. You plan to ask him to relieve you of this mediocre intern.
Head Nurse Sylvia Costa: You are rude and harsh toward her. Why? Because she is just a nurse, and nurses are only fit to assist doctors. However, you must admit that her position as head nurse is well deserved.
Father Tom: You often see him on TV programs, but you cannot understand how he performs miracles. You find it hard to comprehend how he can miraculously cure seriously ill and even dying patients. Maybe he really is gifted—a miraculous presence. You have confided in him, letting him know about your hatred and forgiveness of Hans Rimoret. But now that Hans Rimoret is dead, maybe you should go explain to Tom that you have never killed anyone.
Gail Rimoret: Hans Rimoret’s wife, a lawyer who is pregnant. You know little about her. You have never seen the kidnapper before.
Truths You Must Reveal
Here are some facts that you must carefully reveal when interacting with players. When other players bring up these topics, you must do so.
1.	When speaking of misfortune in your life, you must talk about God.
2.	You cannot tolerate incompetence because you consider yourself one of the world’s top ten surgeons. You work in the ER rather than a private clinic for the rich because of the stimulation and challenge of the emergency room.
3.	You boast that you have never made a medical error in your twenty-year career.
4.	You believe that no patient is unsavable, even if they have already stopped breathing.
5.	You admit to having seen a psychiatrist (for reasons that only concern yourself).

Congratulations, you are the corpse!
Character Introduction:
Don’t be surprised—this is easy to explain. You took advantage of your twin brother Drake Rimoret’s tragedy; you are actually Hans Rimoret. You didn’t kill him, but you did take his place. You have successfully made everyone believe that the one who died was Hans Rimoret. However, you did have motives to replace your brother.
You and Drake Rimoret are twins; you were born three minutes earlier than him. Ever since you were children, Drake Rimoret copied your every move: if you bought a bike, he would buy one too; if you broke up with a girl, the next week he would ask her out; if you decided to become a doctor, the next day he would want the same. His admiration always made you somewhat proud, but the only problem was, Drake Rimoret never had your talent—there was always a three-minute gap between you.
In 2002, you started studying medicine. Naturally, Drake Rimoret followed, and you both chose the most difficult specialty: surgery. Drake Rimoret lacked the courage and talent required for a surgeon. When you realized this, you advised him to focus on research. As always, Drake Rimoret did as you said. From childhood, you two were never apart, and although you could be cold and tough toward him, deep down you loved your brother.
Soon, you started your surgical internship under the guidance of Doctor Andrew Palowski—not an easy experience. For interns, Doctor Andrew Palowski was notoriously hard to get along with. But thanks to his guidance, you finished your studies at the top of your class. For Drake Rimoret, the same training was much harder and more frustrating, but with your help, he still became a doctor. Afterwards, you decided to work in the emergency department at Birmingham Hospital, as the intensity and pressure excited you. To keep Drake Rimoret by your side, you convinced the chief administrator, Richard Berkeley, to hire Drake Rimoret and place him in the same department as you. So Drake Rimoret continued to practice medicine, though he spent most of his time in the lab. Years passed, and both of you were happy with this life.
In 2003, you met the second most important person in your life—Gail Rimoret. She came to Birmingham Hospital seeking advice for her father’s heart condition, and you both noticed each other instantly. It was love at first sight. Within a year, you were married, and she became Gail Rimoret. Your best man was Drake Rimoret, who was genuinely happy for you. A few months later, Gail Rimoret became a lawyer, graduating first in her class.
Now, Gail Rimoret is pregnant! This made you the happiest person in the world, and it delighted Drake Rimoret as well, since for the first time, he would become an uncle.
At work, Drake Rimoret rarely told you about his research, and honestly, you weren’t interested. However, a few weeks ago, he quietly confided in you that he had synthesized a drug that could make him incredibly rich. He didn’t say much more (Drake Rimoret is a bit superstitious). At first, you were happy for him, but then you began to resent your own fate: why him? Why should someone less talented than you become rich, while you earn a modest salary, and he—just by luck—discovers a chemical compound and is about to roll in money? Still, Drake Rimoret was your brother, and such petty thoughts were not enough for you to harm him or steal his identity.
The real reason you decided to take Drake Rimoret’s place was because of your work. A few months ago, a boy injured in a car accident was brought into the ER with severe abdominal trauma. After basic treatment, he was sent to the operating room. You found that one of his kidneys was badly damaged and prepared to remove it; the other organs were in bad shape too, but you were confident you could save him. In the end, you did save him, but this became a turning point in your life. A month later, that boy (Jim Mason) sued both you and the hospital over the removal of a kidney he claimed was unnecessary. Even though your wife was a lawyer, you wisely handed the case to her colleague—you didn’t want Gail Rimoret to be disappointed in you. Meanwhile, the hospital washed its hands of responsibility, leaving you and Jim Mason to sort it out.
Last week, you met with the lawyer, who told you: "We have only a one-in-ten chance of winning." If your mistake was confirmed, your career would be ruined and you would be infamous for life. For days, you were tormented about how to escape this situation. So, when a perfect opportunity arose—one that would get you out of trouble and let you continue your beloved career—you seized it without hesitation.
Tonight, at 6:45 pm, you finished your shift. Before leaving, you went to the lab to say goodbye to Drake Rimoret. There, you found Drake Rimoret lying on the ground, not breathing. You tried to revive him, but it was too late—Drake Rimoret had been murdered. Perhaps because you’d seen so much death in your work, you stayed remarkably calm and began thinking about how to handle the situation. After all, Drake Rimoret was dead, and there was nothing you could do. But you could take his place; this would solve all your problems and bring you wealth. You had recently taken out an insurance policy, with Gail Rimoret, Drake Rimoret, and Birmingham Hospital as beneficiaries. That meant if you died, all three would receive $6 million. This was a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity.
You had to change clothes. As you moved the body, you were surprised to find that Drake Rimoret was already wearing your clothes! The name tag read "Hans Rimoret." You didn’t stop to wonder why—you just went to Drake Rimoret’s locker to look for his clothes, but they were gone. You found his blood-stained clothes in the dirty laundry basket. Not caring about their condition, you changed immediately. Now, you had become Drake Rimoret instead of Hans Rimoret.
Now you had to deal with the final detail: the only physical difference between you and Drake Rimoret was a tattoo on your right shoulder blade, which Drake Rimoret didn’t have. Luckily, the bullet that killed Drake Rimoret entered at the exact same spot, making it impossible to tell if there had ever been a tattoo there.
Relationships
1.	Gail Rimoret: As Drake Rimoret, she is your sister-in-law. For now, you think it’s best not to let her know your secret, even though she will be devastated by your (supposed) death. In front of her, you should keep acting as Drake Rimoret, but she is also a key witness if proof is needed that the deceased is Hans Rimoret.
2.	Richard Berkeley: Birmingham Hospital administrator. He was the one who hired both you and your brother. Your relationship is purely professional. In the matter of Jim Mason, he didn’t support you, so you’re still angry with him. But Drake Rimoret was quite grateful to Richard Berkeley.
3.	Doctor Andrew Palowski: He is utterly devoted to his work. You once interned under him. Without doubt, he is one of the country’s top ten surgeons. Drake Rimoret considered him an excellent colleague. But three months ago, his son was brought to the ER after a car accident. You were on duty that night, but failed to save the boy (he was almost dead when he arrived). Since then, Doctor Andrew Palowski has become distant from you (and the rest of the hospital staff).
4.	Elaine Carter: Neither you nor Drake Rimoret had much to do with him; you know little about him except for work. He is Doctor Andrew Palowski’s intern and often complains (as most people who work with Doctor Andrew Palowski do).
5.	Sylvia Costa: The head nurse of the ER, highly professional. You think she is simple, but you are less certain of this since you started working more closely with her.
6.	Drake Rimoret (the one who died, but only you know): Your twin brother, of whom you were proud. You are saddened by his murder and plan to find the killer. But to keep practicing medicine and claim the insurance payout, you have impersonated him, which makes you feel guilty. The only difference between you is the tattoo on your right shoulder blade.
Truths You Must Reveal
Here are some facts that you must carefully reveal when interacting with players. When other players bring up these topics, you must do so.
1.	Your brother Hans Rimoret was an excellent doctor—it was he who inspired your wish to save lives.
2.	Doctors are paid too little, given how much they do.
3.	All accusations against Hans Rimoret are slander—he did everything possible to save Jim Mason; any competent doctor would have performed that kidney removal surgery.
4.	You (as Drake Rimoret) are willing to become a father to Gail Rimoret’s child—he will never know.

Intern, curious, solitary.
Character Introduction:
Your real name is Elaine Carter, and your true identity is a police officer from District 15 Police Department. Your unit has been investigating an organ trafficking case for several months.
It all started with surveillance on James Riley, who was connected to a criminal case involving multiple illegal transactions. He is also closely linked to the organ trafficking case, and your mission is to crack it. He seems to act as an intermediary between suppliers and buyers. During your investigation, you overheard James Riley frequently mention Brighton Hospital in Detroit. You quickly deduced that someone there was involved in organ trafficking. Indeed, a hospital is the best place to obtain such rare goods. Your superior has already prepared for a large-scale operation: he obtained authorization from Director Richard Berkeley for police officers to go undercover at Brighton Hospital.
Your team has a total of eight undercover police officers in the hospital. Because you have some medical knowledge, your superior believed you were suited to the undercover role of intern Erin Carter. In fact, your father is a doctor, and you tried to get into medical school before joining the police. As for your colleagues, they were assigned roles requiring less medical knowledge (receptionist, security guard), while you were assigned to the emergency room. You correctly estimated that the daily stress, constant movement, and chaos (after all, it’s the ER) would make it easier for organ traffickers to operate. In reality, every medical staff member is so busy that it’s impossible to constantly keep an eye on their colleagues’ actions. Administrator Richard Berkeley therefore handed you over to Doctor Andrew Palowski, a renowned surgeon. Doctor Andrew Palowski is very famous in the medical community—he certainly doesn’t know your true identity, so he treats you just like any other intern. Richard Berkeley is the only person in the hospital (besides the undercover police) who knows your real purpose. You have been following Doctor Andrew Palowski’s orders for almost six months now. He is extremely authoritative and has required you to do a lot of work beyond your abilities. You quickly figured out what kind of person he is: a true master of surgery, world-renowned. It’s clear that he wants you to be diligent, focused, and possess solid medical knowledge. Of course, you fall far short of his expectations, which might damage his reputation (there are no bad students, only bad teachers). He thinks you’re not suited for any medical work (which is true—since you have no medical education!), but overall, your disguise as an intern is quite convincing. Although you are a poor intern, you are at least an intern.
Despite being overloaded with medical work, you have never forgotten that your main task is to find anyone in the ER who might be involved in organ trafficking. You regularly report to your superior at the police department. Every day, you carefully observe your colleagues. Over the past six months, you have learned a lot of different things:
1.	Doctor Andrew Palowski recently lost his son. About a month before you joined the ER, the young man became the victim of a traffic accident. You talked to Doctor Drake Rimoret to learn the details of the death, but he avoided the topic. Through your communication, you think that a transplant could have saved him.
2.	Hans Rimoret (the deceased) and a patient named Jim Mason are in a lawsuit (the trial is scheduled for next month). The latter accuses the doctor of performing an unnecessary operation and removing one of his kidneys! However, this isn’t enough to prove that Doctor Hans Rimoret is an organ trafficker. You need to gather more information from other doctors.
3.	Head Nurse Sylvia Costa has also faced a lawsuit: she was accused of causing a patient’s paralysis by injecting a double dose of medication. Thanks to the efforts of lawyer Gail Rimoret, she was proven innocent. Gail Rimoret argued that everything in the ER—including mistakes—happens at a fast pace, and errors are understandable (even after spending just a few months in the ER, you can fully understand this). After seeing Sylvia Costa’s excellent record, the judge agreed with Gail Rimoret’s view and declared it a tragic accident. The paralyzed patient received state compensation, and Sylvia Costa was only suspended from work for a few days.
4.	Father Tom often comes to the hospital to perform miracles. You notice that every time he visits, he talks to Drake Rimoret. In addition, the last two times he came, two patients respectively had their lungs and kidneys removed. Is this a coincidence?
5.	Lastly, today Doctor Drake Rimoret caught your attention. Although he spends most of his time in his lab, he occasionally performs surgery, and you happened to participate in one. At 5 p.m. today, a young man named Eric was stabbed in the abdomen. Drake Rimoret managed to save his life. Before leaving, he made a seemingly casual remark: “At least this patient kept all his organs.” Maybe it was just an offhand comment, but you have to check.
Another problem arose out of the blue: tonight you met Gail Rimoret, the wife of Hans Rimoret, for the first time. You know who she is, but you have never met her. The thing is, a few months ago, when you were finishing your report at the police station, she came to discuss a case with your colleagues. This wasn’t unusual since she’s a lawyer. At that time, you didn’t know who she was, but she had enough time to observe you. If she recognizes you, she will expose your identity. If you have to talk to her, make sure she doesn’t remember you, or find a good reason if she brings up the topic.
But now, other things are happening in the ER: a murder and a hostage situation. At the crime scene, you need to identify the murderer, and of course, find out whether this is related to the organ trafficking case you are investigating. As for the kidnappers, two things limit your actions: first, you have no weapon in the hospital; second, you must ensure that your true identity is never revealed under any circumstances. If your cover is blown, a year’s work will be wasted. From this moment on, hide yourself carefully and watch events unfold.
Relationship with Other Characters:
1.	Doctor Andrew Palowski: He is very strict with you and often keeps you extremely busy. He doesn’t seem to like you, because he doesn’t like incompetent people.
2.	Hans Rimoret (the deceased): He was a good doctor, about to gain a reputation like Doctor Andrew Palowski, but since you only recently joined the department, you know almost nothing about him.
3.	Drake Rimoret: Hans Rimoret’s twin brother, more passionate about research than his actual job, spending a lot of time in the lab. He is sympathetic to patients and friendly to nurses (even though your skills are poor, he has never said a bad word to you). He seems to appreciate you.
4.	Head Nurse Sylvia Costa: The nurse who runs the ER treats you almost like a parent. She is kind, caring, and professional. She quickly noticed Doctor Andrew Palowski’s harshness towards you, so she always helps you whenever she can. Thanks to her help, you have time to conduct your investigation.
5.	Hospital Director Richard Berkeley: He is the only person in the hospital who knows your true identity and mission. Tonight is the first time you have seen him in the ER... He is usually busy managing the hospital. You have never considered investigating him (after all, there is no evidence proving his innocence), but now it seems you can start.
Medical Records:
As a hospital staff member, you can access medical records. However, you are not a real doctor, so you cannot understand the medical terms or translate them into plain language. However, to convince other players that you are a real doctor or to look up a patient’s record, tell the organizer the patient’s name, and they will provide you with the record, but you won’t understand its contents. You will need to rely on your imagination and persuasion.
Truths You Must Reveal
Here are some facts that you must carefully reveal when interacting with players. When other players bring up these topics, you must do so.
1.	Your father is a pediatrician, and you followed in his footsteps into medical school. You are not the most gifted student, but you can see your own weaknesses.
2.	You believe the hostage-taker will soon be caught because you blindly trust the Detroit police (you have a good reason!).
3.	You want to learn about the people you work with, so you ask them many questions, even if it makes you seem gossipy.
4.	You care a lot about surgery, especially about organ transplantation. Whenever the conversation turns to this topic, you will join in.

Character Introduction
You are the elder brother of Tony Bigelli. After your parents passed away, it was you who raised and took care of him.
Both of you make a living through various scams arranged by your godfather, James Riley. One day, while you were transporting a crate of drugs for James Riley, Tony Bigelli was caught and sentenced to six years in prison, while you managed to escape. However, you were injured during the escape and lost your eyesight. You immediately began searching for ways to earn money to hire a lawyer for Tony Bigelli.
It was at this time that Father Tom hired you—he was looking for a stooge for his performances. The job was simple and well-paid: Tom wanted you to pretend to have regained your sight on a television program. You didn’t tell Tony Bigelli about this because he is a very honest person, and you also had an agreement with Tom not to reveal this information. The payment from this job was enough to hire a lawyer to help your brother reduce his sentence. As a result, Tony Bigelli spent less than a year in prison.
A few weeks after his release, you were "miraculously" cured by Father Tom in front of millions of viewers. Your brother began to believe that Tom truly had healing powers.
A few weeks ago, the two of you were secretly hired online to steal files from the central computer at Brighton Hospital. During the job, you were injured, and your brother took you to the emergency room and had to take doctors hostage...
It’s possible that the person who hired you is among the people here tonight. Unfortunately, you only know their online username, and due to the severity of your gunshot wound, you cannot fully remember the name—only that it’s the name of a bird. You told your brother to carefully use some bird names in conversation to observe people’s reactions.
Relationships
1.	Tony Bigelli: Your younger brother, who admires you and always trusts your advice.
2.	Father Tom: Together with him, you deceived your brother. It’s best to make sure Tony Bigelli doesn’t find out.
3.	Hospital staff: You don’t know them, but you must do everything you can to make sure they are not harmed during this hostage crisis.

Fallen in love, determined, pregnant.
Character Introduction
Congratulations, you are the murderer!
In fact, you really did kill your husband, Doctor Hans Rimoret. For the past three years, you have been having an affair with his brother, Drake Rimoret. You fell in love with Drake Rimoret, secretly hoping to live together with him. The problem was, you didn’t marry the twin you loved, but instead married Hans Rimoret. Tonight, you solved that problem.
You met Hans Rimoret because of your father. In 2003, you visited Brighton Hospital’s ER when your father was admitted for heart disease. You were immediately attracted to Hans Rimoret’s physique at first sight. Just days after you started your relationship, you learned he had a twin brother named Drake Rimoret. When you met Drake Rimoret’s eyes, you sensed that something might happen between the two of you. But you met Hans Rimoret first, and that’s why you didn’t dare to deceive your fiancé.
A year later, you married Hans Rimoret. At that time, you couldn’t be sure of your feelings for Drake Rimoret, thinking it was just a fantasy that would eventually fade away. Besides, Hans Rimoret was a better doctor than Drake Rimoret and could earn more money. In short, between the two brothers, Hans Rimoret was the better choice.
Your first year of marriage was wonderful—Hans Rimoret was practically perfect: loving and attentive. But soon, the novelty faded, and every time you saw Drake Rimoret, you asked yourself why you didn’t marry him. Eventually, you gave in to the affair. Hans Rimoret never suspected anything, and even the neighbors couldn’t tell whether the man kissing you on weekends was Drake Rimoret or Hans Rimoret attending a conference. In some ways, you could even be open about your adultery.
Nine months ago, you became pregnant, and your due date is in just six days! You’re certain Drake Rimoret is the father of your future child. However, the child’s appearance will be indistinguishable. After all, the twins look exactly alike, except Drake Rimoret has a small tattoo on his upper back. You have already told your lover that he is the real father. During that conversation, you were going to tell him you wanted to leave Hans Rimoret and live with him, but you quickly realized that Drake Rimoret admired his big brother too much (who was born three minutes earlier).
Drake Rimoret could never tell Hans Rimoret he had betrayed him, not only having an affair with his sister-in-law but also fathering a child. Drake Rimoret doesn’t want to hurt Hans Rimoret! For now, to keep Drake Rimoret, you have to respect his wishes.
The year you got married, you earned your law license with top marks. After trying out various areas of law, you started working for a big firm in Detroit and specialized in criminal law. You’ve had to defend rapists, kidnappers, and all sorts of killers. Violence no longer shocks you. Before this, you even handled a medical malpractice case for your husband’s colleague. At his request, you defended Head Nurse Sylvia Costa. Sylvia Costa faced an unfair accusation when a patient (Robert Casey) was paralyzed after being injected with a double dose of medication. That was two years ago. You don’t remember the details, but you were able to prove the head nurse’s innocence. Your defense was based on the idea that all ER staff are under extreme pressure, and mistakes can happen at any moment and extremely quickly. But such cases weren’t a challenge for you, so you chose to focus on criminal law.
However, four months ago, a colleague told you about another medical case, privately, for a reason. This one involved your husband. Hans Rimoret was being sued by someone named Jim Mason. Jim Mason was brought to the ER after a car accident, with severe abdominal injuries. At the time, Hans Rimoret believed it was necessary to remove one of the young man’s kidneys. But a month later, Jim Mason sued the hospital, claiming the removal was unnecessary. According to your colleague, Hans Rimoret had clearly made a grave mistake, and his chances of winning the case were very slim. Grateful for your colleague’s honesty, you promised to be careful. So, Hans Rimoret hid the trial from you. If the accusation stuck, he would risk losing his job. He told you nothing, perhaps because he was afraid to disappoint you. Indeed, Hans Rimoret loved you, you’re sure of that. The best proof is that he took out a life insurance policy with you as the beneficiary. A colleague who specializes in these contracts told you that if Hans Rimoret died, his brother Drake Rimoret, Brighton Hospital, and you would each receive $2 million—$6 million in total! It’s a lot of money, since Hans Rimoret was an excellent doctor.
But your love for Hans Rimoret was not mutual: you loved Drake Rimoret more than Hans Rimoret. Drake Rimoret is more sensitive, gentle, and loving than his brother. Hans Rimoret is sometimes too cold and overconfident. You decided you had to get rid of your husband… It was a hard choice, but you resolved to murder your husband. Without Hans Rimoret, you could be with Drake Rimoret, and your son would be raised by his real father. And with the insurance payout, you would all be rich… From the child’s perspective, if you married Drake Rimoret, people would see it as more or less normal. However, since Drake Rimoret deeply loves his brother, he would never forgive you if he knew you killed Hans Rimoret. That’s why you had to act alone, in secret, and commit the perfect crime.
And if the worst happens and you’re caught, a good lawyer can quickly get you out of trouble. You know it’s possible—you’ve helped many obviously guilty people go free. You obtained an unregistered gun from an old client (a dirty crook named James Riley)—you do have some underworld contacts because of your work. You have enough leverage on him to make sure he won’t betray you. You included this gun in your plan tonight.
Tonight
Tonight, you and Hans Rimoret were invited to a friend’s house, so you went to his workplace to pick him up. When you got to the hospital parking lot, you took the gun from your glove compartment and hid it in your purse. You put on a pair of gloves (which matched your evening dress and wouldn’t attract attention), then went to find Hans Rimoret in the hospital. It was about 6:30 p.m. No one saw you enter, and you carefully stayed out of sight of the security cameras. You found Hans Rimoret in the changing room as he was putting on a clean lab coat (he tossed the dirty one in the laundry basket). You discreetly followed him back to his lab. When he was about to go to the pharmacy, you took the gun out of your bag and shot him twice from behind.
The lab was sealed, so no one should have heard the shots. He fell to the ground and died instantly. You wanted to check if he was really dead, but you noticed a security camera above your head. From its angle, it didn’t record you, but it also stopped you from getting close to the body. You quickly returned to the parking lot and tossed the gun near a trash can (an empty gun, just in case… the bullets were thrown in the trash), just as a fleeing killer would dispose of the murder weapon. You calmed yourself, as if nothing had happened, and walked back into the hospital, pretending it was your first time there today. This time you made sure you were seen on camera, but you noticed the small red light was off… Had it been off when you acted? You were too nervous to think it through… You went to the cafeteria to chat with your husband’s colleagues and waited for him...
Relationships
1.	Drake Rimoret: He is your lover and the real father of your child, and he knows it. He doesn’t know you murdered his brother, and he must never find out. True, Hans Rimoret’s death made it possible for you two to be together, but Drake Rimoret would never forgive you if he knew the truth.
2.	Head Nurse Sylvia Costa: You defended her in court, and she was clearly innocent. You know nothing about her private life.
3.	Elaine Carter: This is your first time meeting her. Although you rarely visit the ER, you feel you’ve seen her before at the District 15 police station, so you should ask her.
4.	Father Tom: You only know him through his hugely popular TV show, “The Tom Show.” His business is commercial performance, but you can’t help but wonder if he really does have miraculous healing powers. You don’t know the other people present tonight.
Truths You Must Reveal
Here are some facts that you must carefully reveal when interacting with players. When other players bring up these topics, you must do so.
1.	In your work, you don’t just associate with clean or innocent people.
2.	The best thing for your child is to believe that Drake Rimoret is his father—your child’s happiness is more important than anything.
3.	You know about your husband’s lawsuit with Jim Mason, since it’s obvious he had no reason to remove Jim Mason’s kidney.
4.	Sometimes you regret marrying Hans Rimoret—maybe it was a mistake… In fact, you think maybe you weren’t right for each other, but that’s not a reason to kill him.

Hardworking, efficient, opportunistic
Character Introduction:
You were originally a doctor at Detroit’s Brighton Hospital. In 2003, after more than a decade of service, you were appointed as the hospital’s director. Although the hospital is public, you’ve done your best to manage it so efficiently that it almost runs like a private clinic. You try your best to stick to your budget, but unfortunately, sometimes you’ve had to let some doctors go and discontinue certain services.
You enjoy luxury and money, which is why a careful examination of the hospital’s books would reveal that you have labeled some of your personal expenses as hospital expenses. While this is not entirely illegal, it’s definitely a bad habit.
With some experience and help from a lawyer friend, you have become something of an expert in embezzling funds. You know how to be cautious and take every possible precaution, but you suspect the IRS is now onto you. In fact, a few months ago, a police officer from District 15 came to visit. While he didn’t say so directly, he made it clear that Brighton Hospital was implicated in an organ trafficking case. To catch whoever was boosting their monthly bonus by selling patients’ organs, he needed your authorization to place undercover officers among your staff. To clear the hospital’s name, you agreed to his request. In reality, you had no choice...
Deep down, however, you believe the organ trafficking story is just an excuse. Their real goal is to keep you under closer surveillance and find evidence of your embezzlement. You have never heard of any organ trafficking, nor has your head of security mentioned it, or even hinted at it. You suspect the IRS may already be working with the District 15 police. They are not going to make things easy for you...
To avoid drawing suspicion from the IRS, you agreed to let eight police officers be assigned to the hospital. One of them is Elaine Carter. Since he has some medical knowledge, you assigned him as an intern to Doctor Andrew Palowski. This serves a dual purpose: on the surface, you fully cooperate with the police, but since Doctor Andrew Palowski always considers interns to be lazy, Elaine Carter would be too busy handling the doctor’s demands to have time to investigate you. Plus, no one would question whether Elaine Carter is really an intern, because to Doctor Andrew Palowski, all interns are useless by definition (and this Elaine Carter will just seem even more useless than most). As for the other undercover officers, you’ve found them suitable jobs (receptionist, security guard, etc.), and you’ve told your own security staff to keep a close watch on them. Of course, you are forbidden to reveal the police officers’ real jobs or purpose in the hospital. Now, with the police watching both you and the hospital, you have to temporarily stop shifting hospital funds around. Still, you have no intention of giving up your lifestyle—you enjoy living in luxury. To achieve your goals, you’ve found a perfect solution...
As the hospital director, you are one of the few people with access to the latest reports from the lab. While reviewing a report by Doctor Drake Rimoret, you realized he was about to synthesize a revolutionary molecule! In fact, Drake Rimoret has spent years studying Cyclosporin, a molecule used to reduce long-term organ transplant rejection. The only problem is, its effectiveness is just 70%. Now it seems that Drake Rimoret has isolated a molecule from Cyclosporin that can increase effectiveness to nearly 100%.
Undoubtedly, this discovery could sell for millions worldwide. It would make both the inventor and the hospital fabulously rich. Perhaps having Drake Rimoret bring fortune and fame to Brighton Hospital would be enough for you (you could sell products at every medical expo), but if you were the one credited with the invention, that would be even better! So, you planned to seize control of this discovery. No one would be surprised if you made a breakthrough in your spare time. You paid $50,000 (with $25,000 up front) to hire thieves to steal the formula from the hospital’s central lab safe (only lab staff know the code, for security). The lab is on the sixth floor of the hospital. You don’t know the identity of the thieves, and they don’t know yours. All contact was done online through a special anonymous network. In theory, the operation should start before 9 p.m. tonight (at 9 p.m., a complex security system is activated in the lab).
The thieves must also upload a virus to the hospital’s server to destroy the data Drake Rimoret has created. You don’t have access to all of Drake Rimoret’s files (since you don’t have the password), but you know he locks his files in the safe every night. Those files are enough for you to study his results and find the molecule. Finally, as extra insurance, you hired another anonymous individual to burn down Drake Rimoret’s house, so that whatever files he kept at home would go up in flames. No evidence, no proof that Drake Rimoret made the discovery first. Not even in court could he prove it. The fire should break out around 7:30 p.m. tonight, and Drake Rimoret wouldn’t be home; you planned to keep him at the hospital (not to murder him...). You arranged to meet him in the ER at 7 p.m. (it’s best to have a cover story for the meeting in case anyone asks). That’s why you went down to the ER tonight (you rarely go there).
Tonight
You went to the ER to talk with Drake Rimoret. Because you arrived a little early (6:45 p.m.), you decided to get some air. On your way from the parking lot to the ER, you spotted something shiny by a trash can. As you got closer, you realized it was a gun! Upon inspection, you found it was unloaded. Immediately you thought of Elaine Carter. But now, with Hans Rimoret found dead, you’re not sure if you’re hiding a murder weapon. In any case, at 7 p.m. you walked into the café with the weapon in your pocket. You wanted to discreetly show it to Elaine Carter, but things happened so fast you never got the chance. And now, with a murder, hostages, and a weapon you don’t know how to use and cannot use... how will you handle this mess? Technically, the hostage crisis is unrelated to your own scheme. However, a terrible thought nags at you: what if the hostage-takers are the very thieves you hired? The only thing “your” thieves know about you is your online nickname, Vulture.
Relationships:
1.	Hans Rimoret (the deceased): He was a brilliant doctor, but recently got caught up in a lawsuit with Jim Mason. You don’t know the details, but the hospital’s legal department (with your approval) decided to accept no responsibility, so Hans Rimoret had no support at trial. He may resent you for that. He has a life insurance policy, with the hospital as one of the beneficiaries—if he dies, his wife, his brother, and the hospital each get $2 million. Coincidentally, a copy of the insurance contract is in your briefcase.
2.	James Riley: He is actually an undercover police officer, and this must not be revealed under any circumstances. You only met him once, to discuss the intern’s role. He thanked you for your cooperation. You believe you were honest with him and showed him the hospital’s finances. If you need to confide in anyone, it’s him, since he is the only one who knows you have an unloaded gun... But will he believe you when you say you didn’t kill Hans Rimoret, especially since this empty gun could be the murder weapon?
3.	Doctor Andrew Palowski: The hospital’s best surgeon and a role model for everyone. He is extremely demanding of interns, which is why you assigned Elaine Carter to him. You’ve never spoken to him, but you know he lost his son recently. At that time, you gave him a leave of absence to recover.
4.	Sylvia Costa: The head nurse of the ER. You don’t know her well, but you like her because she’s one of the few nurses who doesn’t often complain about working conditions.
5.	Father Tom: You’ve seen some of his TV programs, and you met him years ago at a charity ball in Los Angeles. He convinced you to let him come to the hospital for visits. So he comes frequently, sometimes performing miracles... Although as a scientist you can’t accept his power as real, you have to admit that a few patients have made miraculous recoveries after his visits. Ultimately, you’ve realized that changing the body can also change the mind...
Truths You Must Reveal
Here are some facts that you must carefully reveal when interacting with players. When other players bring up these topics, you must do so.
1.	You love luxury, fancy cars, travel, and expensive clothes.
2.	You spend your spare time doing research, and you’re about to make an important discovery.
3.	As a doctor, you find it easy to talk about the past (you worked at Brighton Hospital for ten years before becoming director).
4.	Managing a hospital is extremely complicated, and sometimes you have to force employees to make sacrifices.

Professional, loyal, reliable.
Character Introduction:
You are the head nurse of the emergency department. You worked hard to reach this position, and you are proud of it. You are highly professional and deeply respected by your colleagues. This job is perfect for you—except for one problem: you don’t earn enough money. Your salary simply isn’t enough for a decent life, and it’s nowhere near enough to cover the expenses of caring for your mother...
In 2005, you learned she was diagnosed with multiple sclerosis. The news was devastating for both you and her—she’s your only family. As an only child, you’ve never met your father, as he left your mother before you were born. Your mother’s illness requires frequent and expensive care. Without treatment, she suffers greatly, and that’s something you can’t bear. You have decided to do whatever it takes to earn money for her treatment.
At first, you tried getting loans from various banks, but you were always rejected. When all legal means failed, you had no choice but to turn to less legal options. You went to a local priest named James Riley, who sometimes lends money. At first, your salary was just enough to pay the interest on time, but soon his demands grew. He insisted you repay the entire loan as soon as possible. You knew he wasn’t joking. You stopped all your own spending to try to make the payments, but it still wasn’t enough—and he couldn’t care less about your mother’s illness. When he threatened her, you realized how serious things had become.
To make it easier for you to repay the loan, James Riley made you an offer—he wanted to involve you in a highly profitable deal: organ trafficking. Given your job, it was easy to guess what he wanted: he wanted you to become his primary supplier! Of course, you refused, but as months went by, you realized your situation was only getting worse. As an added incentive, he promised that after you supplied him with organs, you’d get a cut of the profits. In the end, you took part in this disgusting trade—but for a good reason: to save your mother. Thanks to that justification, you could bring yourself to accept James Riley’s offer.
That’s how you began stealing kidneys, corneas, and livers. Thanks to your job, you had relatively easy access to the hospital morgue, or you could persuade doctors to perform operations. The ER doctors recognize your competence and have always praised your professionalism. In emergencies, they almost never hesitate to follow your information. In fact, except for Dr. Palozsky—who thinks everyone else in the world is incompetent, including you—most doctors appreciate having a highly capable nurse to rely on in complex emergencies.
Seven months ago, you also managed to fool Hans Rimoret. A young man named Jim Mason was brought to the ER after a traffic accident, with severe abdominal injuries and unconsciousness. In the few seconds you were alone with him, you made his kidney look much worse (you injected plasma to make it appear perforated). In the chaos of the rescue, Dr. Hans Rimoret didn’t notice the change, and, with his agreement, you entered the operating room (he likes your help) and reminded him of the need to remove the kidney. He quickly decided to follow your diagnosis and remove it. All you had to do afterward was take care of it. But later, when you heard microscopic analysis proved the removal was unnecessary, you regretted your actions. Dr. Hans Rimoret was then sued by Jim Mason. You deeply regret putting Hans Rimoret in that position, and it might ruin his career. But now, it doesn’t matter anymore—he’s dead...
At this point, you’ve done a good job with the organ trade. You’ve made enough money to live comfortably and pay for your mother’s care. But one day, everything nearly changed. You were supposed to inject an isotope into a patient to prepare them for sleep. The patient was named Robert Casey. After the isotope, you secretly injected a paralytic agent around his brain, making it look like a malfunction. Unfortunately, his body had a bad reaction, causing complete paralysis of his left side. Robert Casey naturally sued you and the hospital. For your defense, Hans Rimoret suggested you contact his wife, Gail Rimoret, an excellent lawyer. She managed to prove that the paralysis was due to a rare allergy to the drug, and that you couldn’t have foreseen it. Luckily, there was no test to show you’d injected the drug. Thanks to Gail Rimoret’s brilliant defense, the judge found you innocent. Still, you believe the paralysis was due to your secret injection...
Apart from that incident, you remain extremely careful. No one suspects your illegal activities—you forge most of the records yourself so no one notices the missing organs. You are still highly professional and never complain to superiors—after all, being transferred out of the ER would be disastrous, since it’s the perfect place for your side business! Besides, you really enjoy your job and don’t want to lose it. Your mother’s condition seems to be stabilizing, and her pain is easing. You intend to keep up the trade as long as your mother needs it.
Tonight
At exactly 5 p.m. today, you helped Dr. Drake Rimoret treat Eric, a man with an abdominal stab wound. You spent over an hour stopping the bleeding and checking his organs. After surgery, Dr. Drake Rimoret told you he needed to change his coat (it was a bit bloody), and as he watched you, he said, “At least this man kept all his organs!” His words shocked you, and you wonder whether it was just a joke or if he suspects you. You plan to talk with him about it briefly.
Aside from that, today was going smoothly—until just before 7 p.m., when you went to the lab to talk to Drake Rimoret about what he said that afternoon. To your horror, you found him lying face down on the floor. You rushed to help him, but he was already dead. When you turned him over, you realized it wasn’t Drake Rimoret—it was his twin brother, Hans Rimoret. You put the body back as you found it and went to notify others working in the hospital. You found Gail Rimoret and Drake Rimoret in the cafeteria. For them, the news was terrible.
Relationships:
1.	Hans Rimoret (the deceased): You truly liked him and regret having manipulated him. You always maintained professionalism, so you know little about his life outside the hospital, except his marriage to Gail Rimoret...
2.	Gail Rimoret: She was your defense lawyer; you worked closely with her during the trial. You’ve barely spoken to her since.
3.	Drake Rimoret: The deceased’s brother. You know he always relied on Hans Rimoret, so Hans Rimoret’s death must be devastating. You need to discuss his comment from this afternoon with him.
4.	Doctor Andrew Palowski: An excellent surgeon, but he always thinks of you as a lowly, incompetent nurse (but to him, all nurses are incompetent...).
5.	Erin Carter: Doctor Andrew Palowski’s new intern. You sympathize with her—she started six months ago and has endured constant criticism and scolding. She isn’t skilled yet, but it’s understandable—working under such a demanding surgeon isn’t easy. You haven’t had much time to talk with her, but you try to help ease her workload.
6.	Richard Berkeley: The hospital director. Since you started working here, you’ve only seen him six times, never for more than ten minutes. This is your first time seeing him in the ER, and now there’s been a murder...
Truths You Must Reveal
Here are some facts that you must carefully reveal when interacting with players. When other players bring up these topics, you must do so.
1.	You think the medical system is a bit feudal. There are two kinds of healthcare: those with money and connections get quality care and good doctors, but for others...
2.	You support organ donation—once you’re buried six feet under, what’s the point of your heart? Organ donation should be mandatory.
3.	Nurses deserve much more recognition in the hospital, especially when it comes to pay.
4.	Losing a brother is terrible for anyone. You don’t know if you could ever handle the loss of your mother—she’s your only remaining family.

by Dr. Palozsky, or a new hormonal therapy that would take longer (up to a year).
You chose the safer, longer treatment. That made Drake Rimoret the only person who knows your secret. For the past seven months, you’ve performed your “miracles” at Brighton Hospital while secretly receiving treatment from Drake Rimoret.
On one of your visits, you met Doctor Andrew Palowski, an outstanding surgeon who wanted to confess to God through you. He told you how he lost his wife and son in a short period and began drinking heavily. He also harbored intense hatred toward Hans Rimoret, whom he blamed for failing to save his son after a motorcycle accident. You comforted him and advised him to see a psychiatrist—just as you do with people whose confessions you don’t really care about. That’s why you’re in the ER tonight, though after what happened at the hospital, those confessions have taken on new meaning...
Tonight, you planned to use your visit to approach Hans Rimoret (your doctor’s twin brother). While studying Drake Rimoret, you also looked into Hans Rimoret’s character—he’s not as upright as his brother and is much more focused on his career and money. You thought he would be the perfect candidate to alter your actors’ medical records. You left a message in his locker asking for a meeting, hoping to persuade him to work for you, but now his death has made that impossible… And now you have an even bigger problem—you know one of the hostage-takers! Two years ago, you hired Frank Bigelli (the wounded one) to fake a miracle of regaining sight on “The Tom Show.” He was blinded by a head injury but spent all his money hiring a lawyer for his brother Tony Bigelli (the armed one) instead of getting surgery for himself. The ophthalmologist who treated him works for you, and in exchange for acting on the show, you secretly paid for his surgery and hired a lawyer for his brother. As per your contract with Frank Bigelli, you never told Tony Bigelli anything, so he truly believes you have powers. He told everyone about your miracle (which boosted your reputation) and remains deeply grateful. But tonight, Tony Bigelli is armed—if you didn’t know the truth, you’d probably be better off, because a bullet can end a life in an instant...
Relationships:
1.	You (character): After visiting Doctor Andrew Palowski, you learned about his loss and alcoholism, and you are considered a person with special abilities.
2.	Doctor Andrew Palowski: A surgeon who became alcoholic after losing his son and wife, and is filled with hatred for Hans Rimoret.
3.	Hans Rimoret: The twin brother of your doctor. You planned to contact him to change your actors’ medical records.
4.	Frank Bigelli: The wounded hostage-taker who was blinded; you arranged for his surgery and hired a lawyer for his brother Tony Bigelli.
5.	Tony Bigelli: Frank Bigelli’s brother, the armed hostage-taker.
Hints:
1.	Drake Rimoret: He is your doctor—very discreet, so for a public figure like you, he’s the perfect choice. Compared to anyone else, he is more trustworthy. He has just lost his twin brother—maybe he needs someone close to talk to…
2.	Hans Rimoret: Drake Rimoret’s twin brother. You never spoke to him, but had considered hiring him—he’s now dead…
3.	Tony Bigelli (you can’t remember his last name): He believes you have a gift, but with Drake Rimoret’s help, you deceived him. He seems very nervous around you (understandably); you feel now is not the time to reveal the truth… If you keep your image intact, you might make it out alive, or even convince him to surrender…
4.	Frank Bigelli: You once hired him to act out a miraculous cure; after you secretly paid for his surgery, he pretended to be blind. You haven’t been in touch for a while, but he’ll support you in displaying your “miracles” when needed.
5.	Doctor Andrew Palowski: One day, he confessed to you and told you he hated Hans Rimoret. Is that enough to make him the killer? Not to mention he might still be drinking—could alcohol have led him to do it? So many questions you need to find answers for…
6.	Director Richard Berkeley: You first met him at a charity ball in Los Angeles. He seems to love luxury. You convinced him to let you visit his hospital frequently. Despite possible negative effects on Brighton Hospital, he agreed. You find him quite charming. You’ve only met the other people tonight in passing.
Truths You Must Reveal
