Here are some facts that you must carefully reveal when interacting with players. When other players bring up these topics, you must do so.
1.	You claim your upright way of life is anything but exciting, but it’s the price you must pay to preserve your gift.
2.	You are proud of all the money you’ve earned, and also find it a headache to hide it.
3.	You’re equally troubled by your overwhelming appeal to women.
4.	You boast that you have cured many patients declared hopeless by modern medicine; sometimes you even claim that with strong faith, medicine isn’t needed at all.

Young, anxious, impulsive
Character Introduction
Your real name is Anthony, but everyone calls you Tony Bigelli. You hate being called Anthony: it reminds you of your parents. You’re a delinquent, making a living through petty illegal activities. At most, you’ve been in jail many times, but your sentences have never been longer than three months. Ironically, you’ve never actually committed any serious crimes, and you’ve never killed anyone. In fact, you always act together with your brother Frank Bigelli, who could be called the brains of your operations.
You have always admired your older brother. After your parents died, he took care of you. You still clearly remember that day: you walked out of school, your parents were supposed to pick you up, but they were late, and you waited alone at the school gate. Then, you saw them appear across the street on the sidewalk. As they crossed the street, a red sports car rushed by and hit your parents. The car started to reverse, but the driver suddenly changed his mind and sped off.
The ambulance arrived quickly and took your parents to the hospital, but it was still too late...
After that, you were forced to leave school quickly and turn to more profitable activities. Because of your Italian heritage, faith has always played an important role in your life. You often go to church. You and your brother make a living from various deals. Frank Bigelli made contact with James Riley, the neighborhood godfather, who deals in loansharking, gun-running, drug sales—James Riley does it all. Most of your jobs come from James Riley. He trusts you both.
Two years ago, he gave you a job: deliver a suitcase to Detroit’s south side. Frank Bigelli was convinced it was full of money. When you stopped at a gas station, the police pounced and opened the suitcase—it was full of drugs! Frank Bigelli had gone to get food and immediately ran. The police chased him, but he got away. Your brother is famous for his "hell-for-leather" escapes. Since Frank Bigelli taught you the first rule—never betray your partner—you were the only one charged. And since you had just turned eighteen, the judge sentenced you to six years in prison! Frank Bigelli immediately came to visit you in jail. But he was already blind! It happened when he fell while fleeing the police, suffering a head injury that left him blind. But as your best brother, he told you it didn’t matter; what mattered was finding the best lawyer to appeal your case. Clearly, he was very worried about you.
Faith in God was the only reason you managed to get through prison. You served your sentence in one of the harshest prisons in the north of the United States. Violence from both guards and inmates was an everyday occurrence. A year later, Frank Bigelli returned for you—your case was retried, and your lawyer (one of Detroit’s best) got you released in three weeks. When you left that hell, you swore never to go back...
James Riley, grateful you hadn’t implicated him, gave you a lot of money—enough to renovate your apartment comfortably...
A few days later, you found out Frank Bigelli had given up on surgery to restore his vision and used the money to get you a new lawyer. Now it’s too late, the doctors say he’ll spend the rest of his life in darkness.
With a last shred of hope, he decided to go on Father Tom’s miracle show.
Tom is a famous televangelist, often performing miracles in front of dozens of cameras and enthusiastic audiences. You’re a fan of his show and truly believe God gave this man the gift of healing. Luckily, Frank Bigelli was chosen by Tom. Deep down, you hoped for a miracle after Frank Bigelli saw Tom. And a miracle really happened! Frank Bigelli was instantly healed and could see! You are convinced this was God’s miracle. The event strengthened your faith. Ever since, you have deeply admired Tom and always carry a Bible signed by him.
Tonight is quite the coincidence—Father Tom and the doctors from Brighton Hospital have become your hostages. You’re certain it’s God’s will...
The reason you are in the emergency room tonight is simple—Frank Bigelli found a new job, supposedly with zero risk to life. But plans never keep up with changes... Someone paid $50,000 (with $25,000 up front) for you two to steal files from the safe in the central lab at Brighton Hospital. You were also asked to upload a virus to the computer center—a real rookie job... You don’t know what was in the files, but $50,000 was enough to keep you from asking questions. You don’t know who hired you, and they don’t know who you are either. All communication was anonymous, through special channels online. The central lab is on the sixth floor of the hospital. You had to finish before 9 p.m., otherwise a complicated alarm system would activate in the lab.
Tonight.
Around 6:30 p.m., you and your brother entered the hospital, carefully avoiding the security cameras. According to the hospital floor plan, you went to the security room, dealt with the guard, and turned off all security cameras in the building. Then you went up to the sixth floor, broke into the safe as instructed, stole the files, and uploaded the virus to the server. Unfortunately, the rookie mission ended badly—a hospital security guard, surprised, opened fire on you. Your brother cried out—he’d been shot! You immediately returned fire, careful not to kill your opponent (you’re not a killer). After a brief exchange, the guard ran out of bullets. You rushed him, disarmed him as quickly as possible, and tied him up.
You turned back to your brother, who had collapsed unconscious on the floor. You managed to revive him, but he had lost a lot of blood. Even though you’re not a doctor, you knew he needed help immediately. In a sudden panic, you figured out which floor the ER was on and took the elevator. The police would arrive soon, but your brother’s condition was too bad—you had to get him treated. As soon as you reached the ER, you knocked over a metal cabinet to block the door, then headed for the doctors’ lab. One last problem—in your memory, you only had two bullets left. You don’t intend to hurt or kill anyone, but if you have to use your weapon, you know you must choose that moment carefully.
Relationships
1.	Frank Bigelli: Your brother. You will do everything you can to save his life. He has a huge influence on you; you believe family is the most important thing. He has done so much for you—you have never thought of giving up on him...
2.	Father Tom: You met this famous televangelist when he restored Frank Bigelli’s sight. You are convinced he has the power to heal. Maybe he can help your brother again now.
3.	Head Nurse: You don’t know her name, but you are sure you’ve seen her at James Riley’s house before. You want to talk to her about something.
Hospital staff: You are meeting all of them for the first time. You don’t know their names or behaviors, but you respect their work and believe in the power of medicine. You do not want to harm anyone. However, if they don’t cooperate or try to contact the police, you do plan to use your weapon to force them to comply. They’ll also have to explain why there’s a corpse lying in the middle of the lab...
Truths You Must Reveal
1.	You firmly believe in the power of Father Tom, and you can even prove it (then you can tell them the miracle story of your brother/sister regaining their sight).
2.	You’ve spent a long time in prison, and you will do anything to avoid going back.
3.	God and family are most important to you—you will never abandon your only relative. You hope this never has to happen. You never had the chance to get an education—let alone go to medical school. The only reason you’re here is your job, and you deeply regret the hostage situation. But you would rather die than be caught by the police—that would mean going back to prison, so you don’t want to do anything criminal.

Your Story
Your full name is [Chitong Zhou]. You are originally from Hefei, Anhui, and were born in the 12th year of the Guangxu Emperor’s reign (1886). Your parents passed away early. Your eldest brother, [Mengdang Zhou], is eight years older than you, and you have depended on each other ever since. Because your ancestors had a connection with the family of [Shouheng Cui], your brother brought you to the Cui family estate, where you were arranged to live in the front courtyard.
When you were eight years old (1893), your brother, unwilling to be ridiculed as a freeloader, trained hard in martial arts and became a guard at the Cui estate. He sent you to the village school—but you didn’t like studying and only wanted to start earning money as soon as possible to help ease your brother’s burden.
The following year (1894), [Shouheng Cui] sent your brother and others to Wuhu to bring [Madam Wang]’s younger sister, [Xiyan Wang], to the estate, arranging for her to live in the north courtyard ([Xiyan Wang]’s room) and assigning her a little maid named [Gan Bao].
A year later (1895), [Gan Bao] angered [Xiyan Wang]. Fearing punishment, she ran away from the estate. Your brother was ordered to search for her and went out to ask around every day.
After some time, your brother finally found [Gan Bao] and brought her back safely. Following the estate’s rules, she was punished for running away and locked up in the south courtyard. Seeing how dirty and skinny [Gan Bao] looked in her old clothes, you felt great pity for her. When you saw your brother locking a wooden board with an iron chain over the well in the south courtyard, sealing the well, you secretly brought water to [Gan Bao] without telling your brother.
[Gan Bao] was only released after promising not to run away again, and she returned to the north courtyard to serve [Xiyan Wang].
At that time, your brother was of marrying age but had no savings. Out of respect for your ancestors’ friendship, [Shouheng Cui] arranged for the lady’s maid to marry your brother—you now had a sister-in-law, and you were very happy.
Your sister-in-law’s maiden name is Yu. She married your brother in the front courtyard of the estate and later brought her younger brother, [Sunian Yu], to live with you. Your brother and sister-in-law lived in the inner room, while you and the eight-years-younger [Sunian Yu] lived in the outer room, and he called you "Second Brother Zhou."
The next year (1896), your sister-in-law was several months pregnant. Your brother, unwilling for the family to remain dependent on others, decided to join the military, saying he wanted to secure a future for his child and asked you to take care of the family. You readily agreed.
After your brother left, you stopped studying. [Shouheng Cui] allowed your family to keep living in your old house, and the lady of the house, out of old friendship, would sometimes help your sister-in-law.
When your sister-in-law was about to give birth, you panicked, and [Sunian Yu] just cried beside you. Fortunately, the lady sent someone to call a midwife, and your sister-in-law safely gave birth to a boy, both mother and child healthy. You now had a nephew.
With your brother away from the estate and you not well-educated, and your sister-in-law illiterate, you went to the lady, who came from a scholarly family, to name the boy. She named him [Lianyi Zhou].
At that time, the lady was also pregnant. After your sister-in-law finished her confinement, she moved with [Lianyi Zhou] to the north courtyard to live in the west room, taking care of the lady while also earning a bit of money for the family. You and [Sunian Yu] stayed in the front courtyard and helped look after him for your sister-in-law. Not wanting to be seen as freeloaders, you went to help in the kitchen, tending the fire and earning a few copper coins. Worried that [Sunian Yu] might get into trouble if left alone, you kept him by your side. Only after [Sunian Yu] grew up a bit and could help you carry firewood did you feel comfortable letting him out of your sight.
[Gan Bao] once thanked you for secretly bringing her water and would sometimes come to chat with you—you had never talked to a girl like that before, and now that she was clean and fair, you became shy around her.
Later, as you got to know each other, you learned that she knew your sister-in-law and called her [Liu Bao] "Sister."
In early winter, the lady gave birth to a daughter, named [Yannan Cui].
In the following years, your brother would return to the estate for family visits each year, but he never got promoted and could only stay for a few days before leaving again. [Sunian Yu] was still young and sometimes went to the north courtyard, where the women lived, to see your sister-in-law and play with [Lianyi Zhou] and [Yannan Cui] without concern for gender separation.
When you were fifteen (1900), your sister-in-law disappeared. The lady asked you to bring [Lianyi Zhou] out of the north courtyard and live with you and [Sunian Yu]. [Lianyi Zhou], unable to find his mother, cried often, and [Sunian Yu] would comfort him.
You remember the last time you saw your sister-in-law was one night when you were in the courtyard chatting with [Gan Bao]. You saw your sister-in-law carrying [Lianyi Zhou] into the room where you and [Sunian Yu] lived. When you returned after talking to [Gan Bao], your sister-in-law had already left with the child. You asked [Sunian Yu] why she came, and he said his sister came with [Lianyi Zhou] to find you, but left without seeing you.
Soon after, your brother returned for a visit but couldn’t find his wife. He became gloomy, went out drinking, and only came back after getting drunk. As you helped him into the inner room to rest, he finally confessed his difficulties—he had earned military merit but couldn’t afford the 200 taels of silver needed for a promotion.
Worried for your brother, you remembered that when the lady had you take [Lianyi Zhou] from the main bedroom, she took pity on you and unlocked a chest under the bed to give you a few taels of silver, saying it was wages for your sister-in-law’s embroidery work for her daughter. You saw the key was left in the main bedroom!
After your brother left the estate, you decided to steal the 200 taels of silver. Of course, you couldn’t tell [Gan Bao] your plan; when you chatted with her, you just said your brother was upset not to see his wife and went to the riverside to drink away his sorrows, vowing to move the family once he became an officer.
That winter, the lady’s younger sister, [Xiyan Wang], married [Shouheng Cui] as his concubine, and the sisters shared a husband.
Sometimes you watched [Sunian Yu], [Lianyi Zhou], and [Yannan Cui] playing together in the front courtyard and heard [Yannan Cui] say she saw her father go to [Xiyan Wang]’s room to find her aunt, staying there late into the night. Her mother wouldn’t let her go to fetch him, but would sigh and have the maid [Gan Bao] carry her to the south courtyard to spend the night. This made you think you could steal the silver when the main bedroom was empty.
The next year (1901), after some observation, you learned that after the lady took [Gan Bao] and [Yannan Cui] out of the north courtyard, someone would bolt the gate from the inside. You slipped into the north courtyard early and hid in the innermost part. Once the lady and others left at night, you snuck into the empty main bedroom, found the key, opened the chest, stole exactly 200 taels of silver, locked the chest again, and put the key back where it was.
The foundation of the main bedroom is about 40 centimeters high, and the small window on the north side is more than two meters off the ground from outside. At just over ten years old, you were small and light, so you moved a piece of furniture, climbed up, squeezed through the window, landed on the courtyard wall, then climbed down to the lakeside north of the estate. Afraid to keep the silver with you, you ran past the estate to the cemetery, buried the silver behind a neglected grave, and memorized the five characters on the broken tombstone, which you copied onto a piece of paper.
Later, the lady took [Gan Bao] away from [Xiyan Wang]. [Shouheng Cui] planned to buy [Xiyan Wang] a new maid. When he went to the main bedroom for silver, he discovered the money was missing from the locked chest.
[Shouheng Cui] first suspected an inside job and searched everyone’s rooms in the front courtyard but found nothing, so he began to suspect the lady. Upset by the accusation, the lady fell ill that night.
[Gan Bao] cared for the lady and decocted medicine for her every day, but she didn’t survive the winter, dying of coughing up blood with a darkened face. [Shouheng Cui] suspected the doctor had prescribed the wrong medicine and sued, making the doctor pay a large sum for funeral expenses.
[Gan Bao] told you about these events, but you lied to her, saying your brother hadn’t returned to the estate that year—in fact, he visited every autumn, and this year you intercepted him outside the estate to show him the note, so he could dig up the silver at the cemetery and use it to secure his promotion.
After the new year (1902), [Xiyan Wang] was made the new lady.
At the end of summer, your brother finally became a "bangdai" (an assistant military officer) and returned to the estate, wanting to take you and [Lianyi Zhou] with him. [Sunian Yu] knelt before him, begging to go as well. Having shared hardships with [Sunian Yu] and [Lianyi Zhou] for years, you both pleaded for your brother to take him too, and he finally agreed.
Before leaving, you went to say goodbye to [Gan Bao], feeling reluctant to part. [Gan Bao] made you promise to keep in touch, and you agreed repeatedly. After leaving the estate, you stayed with your brother at the military camp in Lianghuai, watching him train troops daily. Later, [Sunian Yu] and [Lianyi Zhou] were sent to school, and you found a job at a foreign firm, running errands for the British, donning a suit, and even taught yourself some English.
In the first year of the Republic (1912), men cut their queues, and there were no longer strict taboos on men and women meeting. Your brother, following his superiors, went from being a Qing soldier to joining the new army, and his immediate supervisor became the Border Affairs Commissioner of Yu and Wan ([Sichong Ni]), trusted by the president.
Last year (1913), [Sunian Yu] took a teaching job at a new-style school in Hefei city, and [Lianyi Zhou] stayed with your brother—you know your brother wants to find [Lianyi Zhou] a good job so he’ll have a bright future.
This year (1914), with warlords all over the country, your brother’s superior became General Anwu and, leading the Anwu Army, dominated Anhui. The governor’s mansion he built in Bengbu was called the General’s Office. You heard the General’s Office was about to be electrified and thought it was a chance to make money—at that time, electric lights were called incandescent lamps, a recent invention, glowing as soon as you turned on the power.
You figured [Sunian Yu] must know about these things, so you met him for drinks at Mid-Autumn Festival (October 4) and shared your idea. [Sunian Yu] told you that the filament in bulbs had changed from carbon to tungsten, and the latest spiral tungsten filament was invented by Americans just last year (1913). While talking, [Sunian Yu] came up with a way to make big money—rather than act as a middleman for foreigners, why not start your own factory? Using your brother’s military connections, you could get the General’s Office contract first, then expand sales throughout the province and nation.
You agreed it was a good idea, but wondered where to find start-up funds.
After discussing, you and [Sunian Yu] thought of [Shouheng Cui]—[Sunian Yu] said your brother had been transferred to Hefei, so if he could use his military connections to persuade [Shouheng Cui] with promises of profit and veiled threats, he could secure an investment!
At dawn, you two finalized your plan and split up—[Sunian Yu] went to prepare samples and equipment, while you went to persuade your brother. That same day, you found him and explained the plan; he agreed it could work, and told you to visit [Shouheng Cui] at the estate to sound him out before he joined negotiations.
On October 12, you brought a prepared incandescent bulb to the estate to visit [Shouheng Cui], met him in the reception room, and explained your plan—offering both incentives and subtle threats by referencing your brother’s current status under a powerful warlord. [Shouheng Cui] said he would consider it and asked you to stay for a few days.
You were assigned a guest room in the front courtyard. Thinking of [Gan Bao], you wondered how she was doing, and to your surprise, she came to see you in your room and talked about old times. Now [Gan Bao] had become a concubine of [Shouheng Cui], renamed [Chou Tian]. You felt guilty for not keeping in touch as promised, and were emotional about her being forced into this relationship, explaining that you had never forgotten her over the years. Hoping to impress [Chou Tian], you told her about seeking [Shouheng Cui]’s investment, your brother coming to negotiate soon, and your ambition to become factory director once the deal was done!
[Chou Tian] finally smiled—overjoyed, you were about to talk more about your plan, but [Chou Tian] looked out the window, stood up, and said she had to return to the main bedroom to serve the master. Watching her leave, you felt great reluctance. That night, you dreamed of her visiting you, just as you were about to pull her onto the bed, you woke up, sighing in frustration.
Over the next two days, [Chou Tian] came to visit you in the guest room every day, though only briefly. Longing to spend a night with her, you had to watch her leave again and again.
On the afternoon of October 15, [Shouheng Cui] came to see you in the guest room, saying he had received an invitation from your brother the previous day to meet on a painted boat. You accompanied [Shouheng Cui] by car, passing the estate and arriving at the lakeside, where you saw a three-story painted boat moored at the dock.
Your brother, in plain clothes, came down from the boat and welcomed [Shouheng Cui] to the banquet hall on the first floor. After chatting for a while, your brother asked you to check how [Sunian Yu] was preparing. You left the cabin and saw a hand-cranked generator on the deck, with two long wires leading from the first deck to a tree on the west side of the dock, connected to several glass bulbs (small lamps).
Seeing [Sunian Yu] and [Lianyi Zhou] under the tree, you got off the boat to ask what they were doing. Before [Sunian Yu] could answer, you saw [Chou Tian] approaching with two other women. You immediately led [Sunian Yu] and [Lianyi Zhou] over to greet them.
Act Two:
The Story After the Greetings
[Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] both accepted your invitation, and [Xiyan Wang] nodded in agreement as well, joining you all as you boarded the pleasure boat. You carefully avoided the generator, wires, and tools installed at the front of the boat, entered the banquet hall, and met with the elder brother and [Shouheng Cui], who were drinking inside—the elder brother stood up to welcome you. You said you would all watch the incandescent lights together after dark, then began reminiscing about the old days in Cuizhuang. While you spoke, you deliberately glanced at [Chou Tian] and saw her lower her head.
After all, men and women should remain separate, so [Xiyan Wang] soon took [Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] to a small private banquet room on the second floor for a drink, while the men stayed in the main banquet hall. In order to persuade [Shouheng Cui] to invest, you and [Lianyi Zhou] took turns toasting him, and [Sunian Yu] joined in, talking about the prospects of the factory and pouring more wine for [Shouheng Cui]. Under your repeated encouragement, [Shouheng Cui], who was already somewhat drunk, drank even more.
When night fell, everyone left the cabin and stood on the south deck of the first floor of the boat. The cool breeze sobered you all up a little—[Sunian Yu] went to turn the hand-cranked generator, the current quickly reaching the small electric lights, which flashed briefly—they were not dazzling, like rotten grass glowing dimly, unable to compare with the bright moon.
You felt disappointed and looked at [Chou Tian] again, seeing her unsteady on her feet in the flash of light; fortunately, [Yannan Cui] supported her in time. [Chou Tian] said she felt unwell from the wind, so [Yannan Cui] helped her back into the cabin. You heard [Yannan Cui] say she would accompany [Chou Tian] upstairs to rest for a while; you also wanted to be with [Chou Tian], but then saw [Xiyan Wang] enter the cabin, so you followed them inside.
At that moment, [Sunian Yu] stopped cranking, and you followed [Xiyan Wang] upstairs, entered the small banquet room, and saw [Yannan Cui] helping [Chou Tian] sit down. [Chou Tian] then lay on the table. Soon after, you heard a drunken man’s voice downstairs calling for [Yannan Cui].
[Xiyan Wang] pulled [Yannan Cui] outside the private room, leaving only you and [Chou Tian] inside. With a hazy gaze, [Chou Tian] softly said to you, “I’ve drunk too much; I’m afraid I can’t leave now... Come take me home later...” Hearing this, you were overjoyed, thinking your wish was about to come true. You nodded repeatedly, almost laughing out loud.
Just then, [Xiyan Wang] walked in. Fearing she would become suspicious, you left and went downstairs, with [Yannan Cui] following you back to the first floor, where you saw [Shouheng Cui] staggering, his face flushed, reeking of alcohol.
[Yannan Cui] wanted to take [Shouheng Cui] home, and [Lianyi Zhou] came to help. The elder brother asked you to take the lantern and see them off, while [Sunian Yu] was still checking the machines, seemingly also dissatisfied with the results.
Holding the lantern in one hand, and supporting [Shouheng Cui] with [Lianyi Zhou] in the other, [Yannan Cui] followed you as you carefully avoided the generator, wires, and tools at the front of the boat. There was no carriage, so you had to walk back to Cuizhuang... After returning, [Yannan Cui] went to find a servant to place the burning charcoal into the brazier in the master’s room. While [Lianyi Zhou] supported [Shouheng Cui] into the north yard and back to his room, [Shouheng Cui] called for water. You went to the kitchen, fetched a bowl of water, and brought it into the master’s room, seeing that the brazier inside was already lit. [Lianyi Zhou] helped [Shouheng Cui] sit on the bed and took the water bowl from you.
[Yannan Cui] wanted to open the small window in the master’s room but couldn’t reach it, so you brought a chair from the bedside, stood on it, and opened half the small window. Looking out, you saw the deep night, and noticed the other half of the window was nailed shut. After coming down, you worried that [Shouheng Cui] would see the chair by the window when he sobered up and remember the incident of the lost silver, so you quickly moved the chair back to its original place. [Shouheng Cui] called for water again, so [Yannan Cui] took the bowl handed over by [Lianyi Zhou] and gave it to [Shouheng Cui], watching him drink it all in one go.
Even after drinking water, [Shouheng Cui] was still not sober. Regardless of the fact that [Chou Tian] had not returned, after setting down the bowl, he said he wanted to rest. You all left the master’s room, hearing [Shouheng Cui] bolt the door from inside. You knew [Shouheng Cui] was drunk and would probably sleep until dawn. Tonight is the chance!
As it was already late, [Yannan Cui] suggested you and [Lianyi Zhou] stay in the guest room for the night.
After settling you in, [Yannan Cui] headed to the south yard. You were eager to go back and find [Chou Tian], but worried that others—including [Lianyi Zhou]—might notice, so you first told [Lianyi Zhou] to stay in the guest room. Then you chatted with the steward [Uncle Fu] for a while before saying you wanted to return to the boat, taking the lantern and leaving Cuizhuang.
Holding the lantern, you carefully made your way around Cuizhuang. As you crossed the bridge, you vaguely saw candlelight moving towards the main dock—just then, your lantern suddenly dimmed. You quickly checked and found the candle was almost burned out, so you ran back to Cuizhuang, knocked on the main door, returned to the guest room, changed the candle on the candlestick, and left again. When you reached the main dock, you realized you hadn’t seen [Lianyi Zhou] in the guest room earlier.
Not having time to think more, you boarded the pleasure boat, carefully avoiding the items at the front. Inside, you heard [Chou Tian] in the cabin say, “Ma’am, I want to go back now. Will you come with me?” Then you heard another woman reply, “I still have things to discuss. You go ahead.”
By now, you had reached the cabin door and saw [Chou Tian] at the door of the brightly lit banquet hall answer, “Okay, ma’am.” She then turned and left the cabin, coming face-to-face with you.
As you and [Chou Tian] were about to meet in private, naturally you were afraid of being discovered by the others inside, so you both kept silent, carefully avoiding the items at the front of the boat, and got off together.
With you holding the lantern, [Chou Tian] led you to the foot of the deserted mountain, hiding in a cave behind the pavilion. You didn’t expect such a good hiding spot. You put down the lantern and hugged [Chou Tian]. Before you could do anything else, [Chou Tian] began to cry.
You were flustered and kept comforting her as she cried. By the time she calmed down, it was already October 16th. Looking at [Chou Tian]’s tear-streaked face, you seemed to see the dirty little maid [Gan Bao] from years ago, remembering the times she came to chat with you as a boy, and the grievances she suffered in Cuizhuang. Now, with the autumn wind turning cold, [Chou Tian] shivered in your arms, closed her eyes, and soon fell asleep. You felt even more sorry for her, so you hugged her and rested as well, determined in your heart to help
[Chou Tian] break free from [Shouheng Cui] and become your woman!
At dawn, [Chou Tian] woke in your arms. You told her you wanted to take her to ask the elder brother for help. At this moment, you just wanted to keep [Chou Tian] by your side and didn’t want her to return to Cuizhuang.
You then took [Chou Tian] back to the main dock, carefully avoiding the items at the front of the boat, and entered the first floor cabin. You saw [Xiyan Wang] coming down from the second floor, [Yannan Cui] coming out of the main cabin, and [Lianyi Zhou] pulling [Sunian Yu] in the corridor, pointing at the banquet hall and shouting: “My father has been killed!” You couldn’t believe it—the elder brother was skilled in martial arts and usually carried a crossbow and a pistol. How could he be killed so easily?
Act Three:
The Story Afterwards
At noon, after you all saw the elder brother’s corpse, you stayed on the boat, keeping everything on the boat unchanged. Just as you were trying to discuss and reach a conclusion, Cuizhuang’s steward [Uncle Fu] rushed into the cabin.
[Uncle Fu] was out of breath, saying he was looking for the madam, the concubine, and the young lady. When [Uncle Fu] saw them, he first greeted them respectfully, then said that at dawn, he saw [Lianyi Zhou] coming out of the north yard to say goodbye, holding the lantern borrowed from him last night and leaving Cuizhuang in a hurry, seemingly after greeting the master—[Uncle Fu] remembered the master came home drunk last night and didn’t dare disturb him right away.
After sunrise, when [Uncle Fu] still didn’t see the master get up, he went to knock on the master’s room door, but there was no response.
[Uncle Fu] said he tried to open the door at that time and found it locked from the inside. Knowing the master’s habit of bolting the door before sleep, he knocked harder and called out several times, but still got no answer. Only then did [Uncle Fu] panic. Unable to find the madam, concubine, or young lady in the house—having heard they were all invited onto the boat the day before and only the young lady accompanied the master back at night but was now not in Cuizhuang—
[Uncle Fu] dared not make decisions on his own and had to come here to find the madam, concubine, and young lady to return and decide what to do.
After hearing this, [Xiyan Wang] took [Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] home, with you, [Sunian Yu], and [Lianyi Zhou] accompanying them, following [Uncle Fu] back to Cuizhuang. Entering the north yard, you again tried knocking on the master’s room door, but there was still no response.
[Yannan Cui] was anxious and asked [Uncle Fu] to quickly find someone to break open the door. Before [Uncle Fu] could call for the servants, you, [Sunian Yu], and [Lianyi Zhou] worked together to force the door open. As the bolt broke, the door finally opened. You all went in and saw [Shouheng Cui] lying on his side on the bed, his face still flushed as if from alcohol, but completely motionless. You went up and touched him, realizing [Shouheng Cui] was no longer breathing, and hurriedly told everyone that [Shouheng Cui] was dead and his body had already gone cold.
[Yannan Cui] immediately began to cry, [Chou Tian] held her in her arms, and [Xiyan Wang] was pale with fright. You looked around and saw that the small window was still open from the inside, the brazier was overturned by the wall, and there was an empty bowl on the table by the bed.

Your Story
Your surname is Sun, and your childhood name is Tian’er. You were born in the twelfth year of the Guangxu Emperor’s reign (1886) in Chaohu, and your family lives to the north of the barren mountain. Your parents died young, and your elder brother [Xianen Sun], who is ten years older than you, worked as a boatman on a pleasure barge on the lake to support you. Sometimes he would bring back delicious food from the boat in a lunchbox for you.
When you were eight years old (1893), your brother borrowed money from Cui Village to get married, accumulating a debt. Not long after his marriage, the guard of Cui Village, [Mengdang Zhou], came to your house to collect interest. Seeing that your brother could not pay, he brought [Granny Zhang] to your home, who took you away and sent you to the creditor’s household to work as interest payment.
[Granny Zhang] said that your family would never be able to pay off the debt and suggested selling you to a pleasure barge, which could at least bring back some silver. Fortunately, the lady’s ([Madam Wang]’s) maid, [Liu Bao], spoke on your behalf, so you were kept— the lady changed your name to [Gan Bao] based on your childhood name and made you a maid. At that time, you relied on Sister [Liu Bao] to care for you. She taught you how to work carefully and comforted you by saying that once your brother raised enough money, he would come to get you.
The next year (1894), the lady’s younger sister, [Xiyan Wang], was brought to live in Cui Village. The master, [Shouheng Cui], assigned you to personally attend to [Xiyan Wang] and live with her in the north courtyard ([Xiyan Wang]’s room).
Another year passed (1895). As Mid-Autumn had passed and the cold wind began to rise, you still hadn’t seen your brother come to redeem you. You grew increasingly homesick, and while serving [Xiyan Wang], you became distracted. You brought her only hot water for washing her face, scalding her, and she cursed you loudly. You felt so wronged that you began to cry.
[Xiyan Wang] raised her hand to hit you, and you immediately ran out— [Xiyan Wang] chased you into the courtyard, but was stopped by Sister [Liu Bao], preventing the beating. Still, [Xiyan Wang] would not let it go, telling you that she’d beat you to death when you returned! Terrified, you hid behind Sister [Liu Bao], crying and begging her for help— you wanted to go home!
Sister [Liu Bao] tried to persuade you to give up that idea, but this time, you did not listen. You secretly escaped from Cui Village, went to the main wharf, and waited until the pleasure barge docked. When you saw your brother disembark, you ran to him and said you wanted to go home with him. Your brother was overjoyed at first, but then frowned, worried that the creditors would come to your house for revenge if you ran away. He shook his head, saying he could not take you home.
To keep you from being caught, your brother hid you at the foot of the barren mountain, in a cave behind a pavilion. He brought you a few cornbread rolls in the lunchbox from home and a thick piece of clothing, then continued to bring you food every couple of days. You thought things would get better after some time, but [Mengdang Zhou] followed your brother and found the cave. Your brother tried to stop [Mengdang Zhou] from entering, but was kicked to the ground.
Trying to protect you, your brother grabbed a stone from the ground to fight [Mengdang Zhou], but [Mengdang Zhou] was quick and shot your brother in the leg with a crossbow he carried, causing your brother to collapse in pain.
You ran to your brother, holding him as blood from his leg stained your clothes. [Mengdang Zhou] strode over, and though you tried to fight him, he picked you up like a chick and brought you back to Cui Village. Before handing you over to [Xiyan Wang], he locked you in the south courtyard until you were so hungry and weak that you promised not to run away again. Only then did he send you back to the north courtyard to serve [Xiyan Wang].
Afterward, [Shouheng Cui] arranged for Sister [Liu Bao] to marry [Mengdang Zhou]. When you heard this, you went to find Sister [Liu Bao], wanting to warn her that [Mengdang Zhou] was not a good person! Before you could speak, Sister [Liu Bao] hugged you and shared terrible news— your brother’s leg, wounded by [Mengdang Zhou]’s crossbow, became infected because he did not receive medical treatment in time. He ran a high fever for days and passed away last night.
A few days later, [Mengdang Zhou] married Sister [Liu Bao] in Cui Village. You remembered your brother’s death and, seeing Sister [Liu Bao] marry your enemy, you grew angry at her and avoided her. You only heard that she brought her younger brother to Cui Village, and [Mengdang Zhou]’s brother [Chitong Zhou] lived with them. When you were locked in the south courtyard, [Chitong Zhou] once brought you water, as the well there had dried up. [Mengdang Zhou] had locked a wooden board over the well with an iron chain.
The following year (1896), Sister [Liu Bao] gave birth to a boy. Since she was illiterate, she asked the lady to name him [Lianyi Zhou]. At that time, the lady was also pregnant. After Sister [Liu Bao]’s confinement, she moved back to the north courtyard, living in the west room, taking care of her child and serving the lady.
[Xiyan Wang] took you every day to the master’s house to see the lady, so you met Sister [Liu Bao] and, seeing her raising the enemy’s child, you grew more resentful and deliberately avoided her. During that time, you did not see [Mengdang Zhou], and anxious to know his whereabouts but unwilling to ask Sister [Liu Bao], you pretended to thank [Chitong Zhou] for bringing you water and learned from him that [Mengdang Zhou] had left to join the army when Sister [Liu Bao] was pregnant. You often chatted with [Chitong Zhou] after that, seizing opportunities to ask about his brother, and you became gradually familiar. In front of [Chitong Zhou], you still called [Liu Bao] “Sister [Liu Bao].”
In early winter, the lady gave birth to a daughter, named [Yannan Cui].
In the following years, [Mengdang Zhou] returned to Cui Village every year to visit his family, but you never found a chance for revenge.
When you were fourteen (1899), you heard that the lady wanted to find a husband for [Xiyan Wang], but nothing was decided.
A year later (1900), when you went to visit [Chitong Zhou], you found no one at home. On the wall hung a large key, which you recognized as the one that had locked the well all those years ago. You took the key, unlocked the board over the dried well in the south courtyard, then put the key back without anyone noticing. You could never forget your brother’s death and finally realized a truth you should have understood long ago— before you ran away, you had only told Sister [Liu Bao] you wanted to go home. It must have been she who betrayed you. She even married your enemy; she deserved to die!
The next night, while talking with [Chitong Zhou], you saw Sister [Liu Bao] carrying [Lianyi Zhou] to his room, and you made up your mind. You returned to the north courtyard, waited for Sister [Liu Bao] to return to the west room with her child, and called her outside— you deliberately called only “[Liu Bao],” not “Sister [Liu Bao].” When she came out, you tricked her into following you to the south courtyard, then struck her on the back of the head with a stone and pushed her body into the dried well, locking the board over it. After Sister [Liu Bao]’s disappearance, the lady asked [Chitong Zhou] to take [Lianyi Zhou] out of the north courtyard, where the women lived, to live with him and Sister [Liu Bao]’s brother.
Later, you heard from [Chitong Zhou] that his brother came home for a visit, but did not see his wife. He went to the riverside to drink and, after returning drunk, said that once he became a general, he would take his family away from Cui Village.
After the Mid-Autumn Festival, the lady felt unwell and had you help her take care of [Yannan Cui] in the master’s room. The lady never scolded or beat you and even taught you to read, so you tended to [Yannan Cui] with care. Soon after, you heard that [Xiyan Wang] was in her room in great distress. By late autumn, the lady recovered and went to stay with [Xiyan Wang].
After winter set in, [Xiyan Wang] married [Shouheng Cui] as his concubine, sharing a husband with her elder sister. [Shouheng Cui] favored [Xiyan Wang] greatly and, seeing her quarreling with her elder sister and not wanting to live together, allowed her to remain in her room, visiting her at night. The master’s house and [Xiyan Wang]’s room faced each other; at night, young [Yannan Cui] would not see [Shouheng Cui] return home and would cry to go to her aunt’s room to bring her father back. The lady could only sigh and, to avoid confrontation, had you take [Yannan Cui] to spend the night in the south courtyard. [Yannan Cui] kept crying for her father, so you held her tightly, afraid she would get close to the dried well.
When you were sixteen (1901), the lady kept you, and [Shouheng Cui] planned to buy a new maid for [Xiyan Wang]. When he went to the master’s room to get money, he discovered that some silver was missing from the locked chest under the bed and became furious.
[Shouheng Cui] first suspected a thief in the house and searched everyone’s quarters in the front courtyard, but found nothing. He then suspected the lady had stolen it. Upset, the lady fell ill that night, and the doctor said she had caught a chill. [Shouheng Cui], hearing her cough and fearing contagion, made the lady move to the west room. You knew that [Shouheng Cui] was afraid of getting sick from the cold; every year after the Mid-Autumn Festival, he would keep a brazier of hot coals in his room and open a small window to ventilate at night to avoid carbon monoxide poisoning.
[Yannan Cui] did not want to leave her mother, so you took her to live in the west room, caring for the lady and decocting her medicine every day. Even so, the lady did not survive the winter.
The day the lady passed away, you heard that [Xiyan Wang] had ordered red bean rice wine from the kitchen. You mentioned this to the lady, who said she wanted to drink some too. When you went to the kitchen to fetch it, you learned that the master had already taken it all, so you went to [Xiyan Wang]’s room to ask for some. [Shouheng Cui] gave you a bowl, telling you to add some rock sugar and boil it again. You did so and brought it back to the lady.
Shortly after drinking, the lady’s illness suddenly worsened. She began to cough blood and said that if she died, all her jewelry should go to [Xiyan Wang]. Before the doctor could arrive, the lady passed away, her face darkened.
After her death, [Yannan Cui] was heartbroken, and you stayed by her side. Later, while talking to [Chitong Zhou], you mentioned the lady’s death and heard that his brother had not returned to Cui Village that year.
The following year (1902), [Xiyan Wang] became the official wife, taking her new maid ([Gui Bao], purchased by [Shouheng Cui]) and staying in her previous quarters ([Xiyan Wang]’s room).
At the end of summer, [Mengdang Zhou] returned to Cui Village to take away his brother, his son, and Sister [Liu Bao]’s brother. You didn’t know your enemy would never return until [Chitong Zhou] came to say goodbye. You pretended to be sad and made him promise to keep in touch, so as not to lose track of your enemy.
Three years later (1904), you were taken by [Shouheng Cui] as his concubine and moved from the west room to the master’s house, saying he would give you a name unlike that of a maid. Remembering your childhood name and your brother’s name, and determined to never forget his wrongful death, you took the name [Chou Tian]. [Yannan Cui] could not bear to part with you and complained to her father. [Shouheng Cui] was very angry, scolded [Yannan Cui] (who had lost her mother), and made her move out of the north courtyard to the south courtyard, where a head maid ([Yang Bao]) would care for her.
Though you feared the south courtyard, you still often visited [Yannan Cui] there and sometimes saw [Xiyan Wang]. You didn’t dare stay overnight in the south courtyard, but as a concubine, you had to return to the master’s house at night to be with [Shouheng Cui]. Since losing his silver, your father was extra cautious, always keeping the chest’s key on him and locking the bedroom door from the inside before bed, so you couldn’t get in if you came home late.
In the first year of the Republic (1912), men cut off their queues, and strict gender segregation was relaxed.
Last year (1913), you heard from [Yannan Cui] that she wanted to study, so you pleaded with [Shouheng Cui] to let her go to the new-style boarding school in Hefei. After that, [Yannan Cui] only came home during holidays. The head maid who had cared for her moved to the west room of the north courtyard to continue serving the women.
This year (1914), in September, [Yannan Cui] returned home after school and stayed at Cui Village, spending the Mid-Autumn Festival (October 4) with the family.
On October 12, you heard that [Chitong Zhou] came to visit Cui Village. As his old room had become a storeroom, [Shouheng Cui] put him up in a guest room in the front courtyard.
You wanted to find out about your enemy, so you secretly went to the guest room to meet [Chitong Zhou] and chatted about the past, explaining how you were forced to become a concubine and change your name. [Chitong Zhou] became agitated, saying he had never forgotten you over the years, and added that he was going to ask [Shouheng Cui] to invest in a factory and his elder brother would soon come to discuss it. Once it succeeded, he would be the factory director!
Hearing that [Mengdang Zhou] would meet with [Shouheng Cui], you realized you finally had a chance for revenge and couldn’t help but smile. Fearing you’d give yourself away, you quickly stood by the window and said you had to return to the master’s house to attend to [Shouheng Cui]. As you left, you felt [Chitong Zhou] was reluctant to let you go.
In the next two days, you went to see [Chitong Zhou] in the guest room for a short while each day. Seeing how impatient he was, longing to spend time with you, you knew you could use him in the future.
On the afternoon of October 15, [Shouheng Cui] said he was going to the pier to meet an old friend and went to the guest room to find [Chitong Zhou], and they left together. You guessed the old friend was [Mengdang Zhou].
[Yannan Cui] came to the master’s house, pulled you to join her in chatting with [Xiyan Wang]. During this time, you heard a maid say a large three-story pleasure barge had arrived at the pier and saw the master go aboard. Thinking of [Shouheng Cui]’s meeting with [Mengdang Zhou], you said you wanted to go see the excitement, so [Yannan Cui] pleaded with [Xiyan Wang] to take you both out.
[Xiyan Wang] agreed, and after dressing up, the three of you went out together, circling around Cui Village toward the lakeshore, where you saw a large three-story pleasure barge docked at the pier. Two long wires extended from the first deck to trees on the west side of the pier, connected to several glass fixtures.
[Chitong Zhou], dressed in a suit, and two young men stood under the tree. Seeing you, they walked over to greet you.
Act Two:
After the greetings
You see that [Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] both accepted [Chitong Zhou]’s invitation, and you also want to take the chance to see [Mengdang Zhou], so you pretend to be indifferent, nod in agreement, and join [Chitong Zhou] and the others as they board the pleasure boat. Carefully avoiding the generator, wires, and tools installed at the front of the boat, you walk into the banquet hall, meeting [Shouheng Cui] and [Mengdang Zhou], who are inside enjoying drinks.
[Mengdang Zhou], dressed in casual clothes, stands up to welcome you. [Chitong Zhou] says that after nightfall everyone will watch the incandescent lamps together, then starts reminiscing about the old days in Cuizhuang. You don’t want to face [Shouheng Cui] and [Mengdang Zhou] at the same time, so you intentionally lower your head.
After all, there is a difference between men and women, so you then take [Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] to a small parlor on the second floor for a drink, while the men stay in the banquet hall. After drinking some wine in the small parlor, your mood changes—you even feel lucky that you never swore never to see [Mengdang Zhou] again, and memories of your passionate romance come flooding back. [Mengdang Zhou] once said he was skilled with the crossbow, and then took out an arrow, swearing to you that if he ever betrayed you, he would die by his own arrow! Hearing this, you felt sweet inside, snatched the arrow away and hid it, telling him not to talk nonsense, feeling certain that [Mengdang Zhou] would soon come to propose.
When night falls, everyone leaves the cabin and stands on the south deck of the first floor of the boat. The cool wind sobers you up a little. [Sunian Yu] goes to turn the hand-cranked generator, and electricity quickly lights up the small bulbs with a flash—not dazzling, but like glowworms in decayed grass, nothing compared to the bright moon.
The lights dazzle [Chou Tian], who becomes unsteady on her feet, and [Yannan Cui] catches her in time. [Chou Tian] says she feels unwell from the wind, so [Yannan Cui] helps her back to the cabin. Wanting to see if [Chou Tian] has had too much to drink and is feeling dizzy from the wind, you follow them inside.
At this point, [Sunian Yu] stops cranking the generator. You follow [Yannan Cui] and [Chou Tian] upstairs, with [Chitong Zhou] behind you, entering the small parlor. After [Yannan Cui] helps [Chou Tian] to a seat, [Chou Tian] lays her head on the table. Then you hear a drunken male voice shouting for [Yannan Cui] downstairs—it sounds like [Shouheng Cui].
You recall a night a few days ago, when [Shouheng Cui] came to find you and said that [Yannan Cui] was of the age to get married and that the decision must be his. You suspect that [Shouheng Cui] already has someone in mind, though he hasn't said so, and so you take [Yannan Cui] out of the small parlor to ask her if she has anyone she likes.
[Yannan Cui] immediately shakes her head, reminding you of when you yourself were forced into marriage by [Shouheng Cui] and your elder sister. You can’t help but sigh and tell [Yannan Cui] to go downstairs to see what her father wants, while you stay to look after [Chou Tian] and wait for her to sober up.
You actually want to discuss with [Chou Tian] about [Yannan Cui]’s marriage and see what she thinks, so you return to the small parlor and run into [Chitong Zhou] coming out. After [Chitong Zhou] leaves, you walk over and shake [Chou Tian] awake, asking if she’s feeling any better.
[Chou Tian], mumbling drunkenly, then puts her head back down and closes her eyes. Seeing that [Chou Tian] is barely responsive, you know she won’t be sober for a while, and can only walk out of the small parlor in resignation.
You go downstairs, remembering that [Mengdang Zhou] is in the banquet hall, and your heart starts beating fast. Knowing you shouldn’t go in, you still can’t help pushing open the door. You see two songstresses inside serving [Mengdang Zhou].
[Mengdang Zhou] sees you come in and his expression changes, quickly explaining that he just had them come in to change the candles. The two songstresses, hearing [Mengdang Zhou]’s words, replace the candles and then leave the banquet hall, gently closing the door.
Now, with no one else in the banquet hall, you and [Mengdang Zhou] look at each other by flickering candlelight, as if transported back to years ago. [Mengdang Zhou] once again brings up that he has remained unmarried all these years, not to wait for [Liu Bao] to return, but because you are the only one in his heart!
You tell [Mengdang Zhou] not to say any more—you are now [Shouheng Cui]’s wife, and as long as your husband is alive, nothing is possible.
[Mengdang Zhou] has no choice but to change the subject, saying he plans to have his son marry [Yannan Cui]. You also need to calm yourself down, so you and [Mengdang Zhou] discuss the possible marriage between [Yannan Cui] and [Lianyi Zhou], wondering if their birth dates are compatible and that you should have someone check. This topic finally allows you to relax. Then you hear [Chou Tian] outside the banquet hall door asking: “Madam, I’m going back now. Are you coming with me?”
You realize [Chou Tian] is now sober and has come downstairs to find you—she must want to go home. Once again, you know you shouldn’t stay alone, but use [Yannan Cui]’s marriage as a reasonable excuse—you haven’t finished discussing it, so you can’t go yet!
Having found your reason to stay, you reply to [Chou Tian] outside the door: “I still have some matters to discuss. You go back first.”
“All right, Madam,” [Chou Tian] replies and leaves without coming into the banquet hall.
Seeing that you didn't leave with [Chou Tian], [Mengdang Zhou] already guesses your feelings, comes over, and holds you tightly. Lost in [Mengdang Zhou]’s embrace, your heart is in turmoil, but you can’t forget your identity. Conflicted, you can’t immediately accept this forbidden passion, so you push [Mengdang Zhou] away, begging him to give you some time to think. [Mengdang Zhou] sits down with a bitter smile and says he will stay here and wait for you.
As you leave the banquet hall, you look back at [Mengdang Zhou] in the candlelight before gently closing the door. You turn and go upstairs, return to the small parlor, and drink several glasses of old wine in a row—hoping the alcohol will give you the courage to cross the line, but you are still torn. Adding to it, your poor tolerance means that drinking quickly only makes you dizzy. Unable to hold your glass, you slump over the table, sobbing, blaming it all on him ([Mengdang Zhou]). Why did he leave back then, and why did he return now? Everything in front of you becomes more and more blurred by your tears.
It’s not until you hear knocking in the hallway that you open your eyes and realize that it’s already dawn on October 16.
You pull yourself together and hear footsteps in the hallway. Realizing you spent the night away from home, you immediately want to return to Cuizhuang.
Going downstairs, you see [Chitong Zhou] and [Chou Tian] coming in from outside the cabin, [Yannan Cui] coming out of the side room, and [Lianyi Zhou] in the corridor pulling [Sunian Yu], pointing to the banquet hall and shouting: “My father has been killed!”
Act Three:
The story continues
At noon, all of you have seen [Mengdang Zhou]’s body and stayed on the boat, keeping everything on board unchanged, trying to discuss what happened. At that moment, the Cuizhuang steward [Uncle Fu] runs into the cabin.
[Uncle Fu], out of breath, says he came looking for the Madam, the concubine, and the young lady. After seeing you, [Uncle Fu] greets you respectfully, then explains that at dawn, he saw [Lianyi Zhou] leaving the north courtyard with the lantern borrowed last night, hurriedly leaving Cuizhuang, and apparently had already said goodbye to the master—[Uncle Fu] remembers the master came home drunk last night and didn’t dare disturb him.
After the sun rose, [Uncle Fu] still didn’t see the master get up, so he knocked on the master’s bedroom door, but there was no answer.
[Uncle Fu] says that when he tried to push the door, he found it bolted from the inside, knowing the master’s habit of locking the door before sleeping. He knocked harder, calling out for the master, but there was still no response. Only then did [Uncle Fu] panic. Not finding the Madam, concubine, or young lady at home, he heard they were all invited to the boat yesterday. Only the young lady came home with the master last night, but now she wasn’t in Cuizhuang.
[Uncle Fu] dared not act on his own, so he ran here to ask the Madam, concubine, and young lady to return and make a decision.
After hearing this, you lead [Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] home, accompanied by [Chitong Zhou], [Sunian Yu], and [Lianyi Zhou], following [Uncle Fu] back to Cuizhuang. Entering the north courtyard, you knock several times on the master’s bedroom door, but still get no answer.
[Yannan Cui], anxious, tells [Uncle Fu] to quickly get someone to break down the door. [Chitong Zhou], [Sunian Yu], and [Lianyi Zhou] don’t wait for [Uncle Fu] to call for servants—they join forces to break down the master’s bedroom door. With the bolt broken, the door finally opens, and you all walk in to find [Shouheng Cui] lying on his side on the bed, his face still flushed as if from drinking, but unmoving. [Chitong Zhou] goes up to check and hurriedly tells everyone that [Shouheng Cui] is dead—his body is already cold.
[Yannan Cui] immediately bursts into tears, and [Chou Tian] holds her in her arms. You are terrified, glancing around the room, noticing the small window is open from the inside, the brazier overturned by the wall, and an empty bowl on the table by the bed.

Your Story
Your full name is [Lianyi Zhou], born in the 22nd year of the Guangxu reign (1896) in Cuizhuang. Your father’s name is [Mengdang Zhou]. When you were born, the only people accompanying your mother [Madam Yu] were your uncle [Chitong Zhou] and your maternal uncle [Sunian Yu]—the uncle and the maternal uncle are only 10 and 2 years older than you, respectively. When your mother was about to give birth, one was flustered, the other could only cry loudly on the side. Fortunately, the lady of Cuizhuang ([Madam Wang]) sent someone to call a midwife, allowing you to be born safely, mother and child both well.
Your mother had once been a maid for the lady. Her marriage to your father was arranged by the owner of Cuizhuang, [Shouheng Cui]. After your mother married your father, she also brought her younger brother [Sunian Yu] to live with you. Your father and mother lived in the inner room, while your uncle and maternal uncle stayed in the outer room.
When your mother was pregnant, your father did not want the family to keep living under someone else’s roof, so he resolutely enlisted in the army to try to secure a future for you. After you were born, your father was not around, and your uncle was not well educated; your mother was illiterate, so she asked the well-educated lady to give you a name.
At that time, the lady was also pregnant. After your mother finished her postpartum confinement, she took you and moved to the north courtyard, living in the west room—while caring for you, she also served the lady and earned some money for the household.
Your uncle took care of your maternal uncle for your mother, still living with him in the front courtyard. At that time, your uncle was afraid people would mock him for being idle, so he helped out in the kitchen, making a few coins, and worried about the maternal uncle getting into trouble in the house, so he always kept him close. A few years later, when your maternal uncle was old enough to help carry firewood, your uncle no longer had to watch him at all times.
In the year you were born (1896), in early winter, the lady gave birth to a daughter named [Yannan Cui].
Over the next few years, your father returned to Cuizhuang each year to visit, but he never got promoted, and only stayed a few days each time before leaving. Your maternal uncle was still young and would sometimes run to the women’s quarters in the north courtyard to see your mother. You and [Yannan Cui] didn’t have to avoid each other as children, so you played together from a young age.
You and [Yannan Cui] grew up together in the north courtyard, and every time the adults talked about the past, you would remember some things—for example, the story of your birth, which you heard from the adults.
When you were five (1900), one day you didn’t see your mother in the north courtyard, and only saw her return with red eyes late at night.
The next evening, your mother took you to the front courtyard, to the place where your uncle and maternal uncle lived. When she entered, she saw only your maternal uncle and asked him if your uncle was home. He said no. As it was late, you were already sleepy when your mother carried you out. As she talked to your maternal uncle, you fell asleep in her arms.
Your mother did not wait for your uncle to return, but took you back to the west room of the north courtyard. Half asleep, you heard someone calling [Liu Bao]; when you opened your eyes, you saw your mother walk out of the room and she never returned.
You didn’t see your mother all night, so you went to the main room to look for her and were kept there by the lady to eat. At the time, you didn’t know your mother was missing. A few days later, the lady called your uncle over and gave him a few taels of silver, saying it was your mother’s wages for embroidering for her daughter, and asked your uncle to take you out of the women’s north courtyard.
After that, you lived in the front courtyard with your uncle and maternal uncle. Not being able to find your mother, you often cried, and your maternal uncle always tried to comfort you—every time you asked him if your mother had returned, he would go to the north courtyard to check, but there was never any news.
Not long after, your father returned home. When he found your mother missing, he scowled and went out drinking, coming back only after getting drunk. Your uncle helped him to rest in the inner room. When he sobered up, he told you that when he became an officer, he would come back to take you all away, and then he left Cuizhuang again.
That winter, you heard that the lady’s younger sister [Xiyan Wang] married [Shouheng Cui] as a concubine.
Your maternal uncle later went to the north courtyard again to ask if your mother had come back. After a short time, he came out with [Yannan Cui] and brought you to play together in the front courtyard.
You and your maternal uncle heard [Yannan Cui] say she saw her father go to [Xiyan Wang]’s room to find her aunt and didn’t return to the main room that night. She wanted to go to her aunt’s to bring her father back, but her mother wouldn’t let her and kept sighing, having the maid [Gan Bao] take her to stay overnight in the south courtyard.
When you were six (1901), [Shouheng Cui] discovered money missing at home and searched everyone’s room in the front courtyard but didn’t find it, and suspected the lady had taken it. The lady was so upset that she fell ill that night.
You thought the lady was a good person and didn’t expect that she would die of coughing up blood before winter was over. You were very sad at her death and heard that her face turned dark after death. That year, you didn’t see your father and didn’t know how he was.
The following year (1902), [Xiyan Wang] was promoted to be the new lady.
In late summer, your father was finally promoted to assistant officer and returned to Cuizhuang, intending to take you and your uncle with him. Your maternal uncle knelt to him, begging to be taken as well. You and your uncle had lived with your maternal uncle for years, sharing joys and hardships, so you also pleaded with your father, who finally agreed to take him.
You never forgot the day you left Cuizhuang—it was raining outside, and [Yannan Cui] ran out crying, saying she didn’t want you to leave. You had to follow your father, so you promised her you would come back one day!
After leaving Cuizhuang, you first lived with your father in the army camp in Lianghuai, watching him drill the soldiers every day. Later, you and your maternal uncle were sent to school, and your uncle found a job in a foreign firm.
In the first year of the Republic (1912), men cut off their queues and the strict separation of men and women ended. Your father, following orders, became a new army officer instead of a Qing soldier, and his direct superior became [Sichong Ni], the trusted frontier commissioner of the president.
Last year (1913), your maternal uncle went to a new-style school in Hefei city to teach, and you stayed with your father.
This year (1914), warlords everywhere were expanding their power. After your father’s superior was promoted to General Anwu, he led the Anwu Army to dominate Anhui. The governor’s office in Bengbu was called the General’s Office, and from there, he controlled the salt tax, held power, and even sent people to Shanghai to purchase steam generators to install in the General’s Office and provide electric lighting—these lamps, called incandescent lamps, had filaments now made of tungsten instead of the original carbon filaments.
Your father received an order to transfer to Hefei for a new post and brought you along, saying he wanted to find you a good position. After arriving in Hefei with your father, you thought of Cuizhuang by the shore of Chaohu and of your maternal uncle, now teaching in the city’s new school, so you went to the school to tell him that you and your father had come to Hefei.
While talking to your maternal uncle at the school, you noticed a female student quietly watching you from the side—you thought she looked like [Yannan Cui], so you walked over and called her Yan Nan, and to your surprise, it really was her!
After being recognized, [Yannan Cui] nodded in surprise and also felt you looked familiar—you couldn’t hold back your joy, introduced yourself, and said you had just returned to Hefei, promising to visit her at home after you were settled.
You and your childhood friend reunited suddenly and naturally had a lot to talk about, but before you could say much, the school bell rang and [Yannan Cui] had to return to class.
When you got home, your mind was filled with the image of [Yannan Cui] as she is now. You tossed and turned in bed, unable to sleep—she was no longer the little girl who easily cried, but now a graceful and elegant student, and you found yourself falling for her.
You planned to visit [Yannan Cui] at school again the next day, but your father had already found you a job as a clerk in the city’s military garrison. At dawn, he took you to make visits and arrangements everywhere, so you had no free time.
In September, after you got familiar with your job, you finished compiling statistics on the educational funding of all the schools in Hefei at the garrison and finally had some time off, so you immediately went to look for [Yannan Cui], only to find the school was on holiday and the students had all gone home. Unable to see [Yannan Cui], you missed her even more and finally realized you were in love with her!
During the Mid-Autumn Festival (October 4), you spent the holiday with your father. Seeing him looking gloomy and missing [Yannan Cui] yourself, you drank a few more cups and went to bed early.
The next morning, your uncle, with red eyes, came to your father saying he planned to build a factory to produce the latest incandescent lamps and asked your father to use his army connections to secure the business with the General’s Office for supplying the lamps. Once the money was made, they could sell to the rest of the province and the whole country. As for the capital, they could ask [Shouheng Cui] to invest—with your father’s military background, if he spoke to [Shouheng Cui] and promised him good returns, combining both favor and pressure, [Shouheng Cui] would surely invest!
After hearing this, your father thought it was feasible and told your uncle to visit [Shouheng Cui] in Cuizhuang first to test the waters, and he would negotiate afterwards. When you heard your father would cooperate with [Shouheng Cui], you were delighted, and after your uncle left, you told your father about your feelings for [Yannan Cui].
Your father agreed to let you propose marriage, but not yet—he planned to discuss the investment and factory first with [Shouheng Cui]. If [Shouheng Cui] agreed, the marriage proposal would be much easier. Your father also warned you not to mention the proposal before the deal was settled.
On October 13, your maternal uncle came to see your father, saying he was ready with the demonstration equipment. Your father didn’t know much about foreign technology, so he left everything to your maternal uncle, only requiring that costs be kept low.
On October 14, your father asked you to deliver an invitation to Cuizhuang—you met [Shouheng Cui], personally handed him the invitation, spoke properly and respectfully, then took your leave. After returning home, you learned that your father had secured a three-story pleasure boat for entertaining [Shouheng Cui].
On October 15, at noon, your father wore plain clothes and took your maternal uncle and you to cross the lake on the pleasure boat, then docked at the large wharf, and prepared the banquet. In the afternoon, [Shouheng Cui] and your uncle arrived by carriage and were welcomed aboard by your father.
Seeing [Shouheng Cui] at the banquet, you thought of [Yannan Cui] and couldn’t help but feel excited, hoping your father would quickly close the business deal so you could propose marriage. You remembered you would need to match birth dates before proposing, so you took out the embroidered pouch you always carried, with a red slip inside—after your mother had the lady name you at birth, she wrote your birth date on the red slip. When you were young and illiterate, your mother put it in a pouch for you to carry always.
Looking at the red slip, you saw your birth year, month, day, and hour. You first calculated the lunar date for Bing Shen year (1896), fifth month, and suddenly felt something was off!
You recalled your mother once said she married your father only after the Double Ninth Festival (ninth day of the ninth lunar month) in the Yi Wei year (1895). You knew arithmetic and quickly calculated the days, breaking out in a cold sweat—trying to reassure yourself, you thought maybe your father and mother had made a private commitment before marriage, so you were born less than ten months after. In any case, this concerned your parents’ reputation and must never be known to others!
You wanted to find out the truth but, for now, had to hide it—the pouch was embroidered by your mother, so you couldn’t throw it away. You put it back in your room with your father, tore up the red slip, and went to the kitchen on the first floor. While everyone was busy serving dishes to the banquet hall, you threw it into the stove. After all this, you heard your maternal uncle calling you, so you left the cabin and went to the deck.
By now, the boatman had moored the boat, and your maternal uncle asked you to help him install a hand-cranked generator on the front deck of the pleasure boat, then ran two long wires to a tree on the west side of the wharf, connecting several glass bulbs (small lamps)—you guessed your maternal uncle was planning to demonstrate the electric lights to [Shouheng Cui] after dark.
After you and your maternal uncle finished setting up under the tree, your uncle got off the boat to ask what you were doing. Before your maternal uncle could answer, you saw [Yannan Cui] and two women coming over—seeing them, your uncle led you and your maternal uncle to greet them.



Story After Greetings
[Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] both accepted uncle’s invitation, and [Xiyan Wang] also nodded in agreement, joining you all to board the pleasure boat. You carefully made your way past the generator, wires, and tools installed at the front of the boat, and entered the banquet hall to meet your father and [Shouheng Cui], who were already inside drinking—your father stood up to welcome you. Uncle said you would all watch the incandescent lights after dark, then started reminiscing about the old days in Cuizhuang. You occasionally glanced at [Yannan Cui], seeing her turn her head away—as the beauty was right beside you, you decided not to think about the troublesome issue of your background, pretending that the red paper didn’t exist.
After all, there are differences between men and women, so [Xiyan Wang] took [Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] upstairs to a small lounge for a drink, while the men stayed in the banquet hall. Uncle wanted to persuade [Shouheng Cui] to invest, and you, hoping to impress your future father-in-law, both raised your glasses to toast [Shouheng Cui]. Your uncle also joined in, talking about the prospects of the factory and refilling [Shouheng Cui]’s glass. With all of you urging him to drink, [Shouheng Cui], already a bit tipsy, ended up drinking even more.
When it got dark, everyone left the cabin and stood on the south deck of the first floor of the boat. The cool breeze sobered you up a bit. Your uncle went to turn the hand-crank generator, sending current quickly to the small light bulbs, which gave off a fleeting flash of light—they weren’t dazzling, more like decaying grass glowing as fireflies, hardly a match for the bright moon.
You looked at [Yannan Cui], seeing her support the unsteady [Chou Tian]—[Chou Tian] said she felt unwell from the wind, so [Yannan Cui] helped her back to the cabin. You heard [Yannan Cui] say she would take [Chou Tian] upstairs back to the lounge and saw uncle follow [Xiyan Wang] into the cabin.
Uncle stopped turning the crank. Your father whispered something to [Shouheng Cui], then invited him back to the cabin to continue drinking. You stayed by their side, hearing your father softly say, “Let your daughter marry my son.” [Shouheng Cui] burst out laughing, saying he would have his daughter meet her future father-in-law, and called for [Yannan Cui] to come over.
Your heart leapt with joy as you saw [Yannan Cui] and uncle coming down from the second floor one after the other—[Yannan Cui] saw [Shouheng Cui] staggering, his face flushed and reeking of alcohol, and wanted to take him home. You hurried over to help. Your father asked uncle to take a lantern and escort the guests, while your uncle was still checking the machine.
Uncle held the lantern in one hand, helping you support [Shouheng Cui] with the other. [Yannan Cui] followed as you carefully navigated around the generator, wires, and tools at the front of the boat. Not seeing any carriages, you could only walk back along the road.
After returning to Cuizhuang, [Yannan Cui] went to tell the house servants to put burning charcoal into the brazier in the master’s room. As you helped [Shouheng Cui] into the north courtyard to return to his room, [Shouheng Cui] called out for water, and uncle went to the kitchen.
When uncle brought a bowl of water to the master’s room, the brazier inside was already burning. You helped [Shouheng Cui] sit on the bed, eager to show your diligence in front of [Yannan Cui] and her father, and took the water bowl from uncle.
[Yannan Cui] wanted to open the small window in the master’s room, but couldn’t reach it, so uncle moved a chair over from the bedside, stepped on it, and opened half the window, seeing the deep night outside.
After uncle came down and put the chair back, [Shouheng Cui] again asked for water, so [Yannan Cui] took the bowl you handed her and turned to give it to [Shouheng Cui], watching him drink it all in one go.
Even after drinking the water, [Shouheng Cui] did not sober up. After putting down the bowl, he said he wanted to rest, so you all left the master’s room and heard [Shouheng Cui] bolt the door from inside.
Seeing that it was late, [Yannan Cui] let you and uncle stay in the guest room.
After settling you in, [Yannan Cui] headed toward the south courtyard. Just as you were about to catch up to say a few words, uncle called you to go to the guest room first, so you entered but didn’t see uncle come in.
You waited a while longer, still didn’t see uncle return, so you stepped out of the guest room, walked under the moonlight to the gate of the south courtyard, and found it was not locked, so you went in looking for [Yannan Cui].
There were no lights in the south courtyard rooms. You knocked but no one answered. After waiting for a while and feeling the night wind getting chilly, you couldn’t help shivering. Turning around, you saw the old dry well, covered with a wooden plank chained shut, apparently unopened for many years. As you looked at the well, you suddenly heard a sound, got startled, and quickly left the south courtyard.
You didn’t see [Yannan Cui], so you wanted to go back to the boat to ask your father how the conversation with [Shouheng Cui] had gone and whether marriage was discussed.
When you got to the butler’s room, you heard another sound, then went inside to find the butler and asked to leave, borrowing a lantern from him. Butler [Uncle Fu] invited you in, closed the door, found you a lantern in the dark, then went to find a candle and finally lit the lantern after a while.
Holding the lantern, you left Cuizhuang, cautiously retraced your steps, and while crossing the bridge, faintly saw candlelight moving toward the barren hills. You then walked to the lakeside and reached the big dock, only to find the gangplank for boarding had already been taken back onto the boat by the boatman, making it impossible to get on board.
By now it was night. You couldn’t shout, so you walked back to Cuizhuang along the original road, returned to the guest room, and since you had the lantern, didn’t light the candle in the room, went to bed and blew out the candle in the lantern before falling asleep.
On October 16, you got up before dawn and went to the south courtyard again. Knocking brought no response, so you assumed [Yannan Cui] went to see [Shouheng Cui], and went to the north courtyard. Unable to open the master’s room door or get a response to knocking, you had to give up.
