Wanting to see your father, you went to say goodbye to the butler, took the lantern and left Cuizhuang. It was already daylight when you got outside, so you blew out the lantern, walked quickly along the road to the big dock, and saw the boatman setting the gangplank, so you immediately ran onto the boat, navigated past the items at the front, entered the cabin, went upstairs to room C where you and your father stayed, but found no one inside.
You figured your father might have drunk too much and stayed in the banquet hall last night, so you ran downstairs, pushed open the banquet hall door, and saw your father lying on the ground with an arrow in his chest—he was already dead! You were so frightened you dropped the lantern, and then remembered you hadn’t seen your father’s crossbow in room C earlier—your father, who practiced martial arts in his youth, always carried a crossbow and arrows. Yesterday, because he was hosting guests, he left his crossbow and pistol in room C on the second floor.
Panic-stricken, you ran upstairs to knock on room B’s door. When your uncle opened it, you pulled him to the first-floor corridor, then saw uncle and [Chou Tian] coming in from outside the cabin, [Yannan Cui] coming out from room A, and [Xiyan Wang] coming down from the second floor. Before anyone could ask, you shouted toward the banquet hall: “My father’s been murdered!”
What Happened Next
At noon, after you all had seen your father’s body, you stayed on the pleasure boat, keeping everything as it was. Just as you were trying to reach a conclusion, the butler from Cuizhuang, [Uncle Fu], ran into the cabin.
[Uncle Fu] was out of breath, saying he came to find madam, aunt, and miss. After [Uncle Fu] saw them, he greeted them and then recounted that at dawn, he saw you coming out of the north courtyard to bid him farewell, carrying the lantern you borrowed last night, and hurriedly left Cuizhuang, seemingly after greeting the master. [Uncle Fu] remembered the master had returned drunk last night and did not dare disturb him immediately.
After full daylight, when [Uncle Fu] still hadn’t seen the master get up, he went to knock on the master’s room door, but got no answer.
[Uncle Fu] said he tried to push the door, found it bolted from the inside, and knowing the master’s habit of bolting the door before sleep, knocked harder and called out, but still got no response. At that point, [Uncle Fu] panicked. Unable to find madam, aunt, and miss at home—having heard they were all invited onto the boat yesterday, with only miss returning with the master last night but not present now—he didn’t know what to do.
Not daring to act on his own, [Uncle Fu] came here to ask madam, aunt, and miss to come home and make a decision.
After hearing this, [Xiyan Wang] led [Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] back home, and you, uncle, and your uncle also accompanied them and [Uncle Fu] back to Cuizhuang. You entered the north courtyard, knocked on the master’s room door several more times, but still got no response.
[Yannan Cui], getting anxious, told [Uncle Fu] to fetch someone to break down the door. You, uncle, and your uncle didn’t wait for [Uncle Fu] to bring the servants, but worked together to force the master’s room door open. With the latch broken, the door finally opened. You all walked in and saw [Shouheng Cui] lying on his side on the bed, his face still bearing the flush of drunkenness, yet unmoving. Uncle went forward to check and hurriedly told everyone that [Shouheng Cui] was dead, his body already cold.
[Yannan Cui] immediately burst into tears. [Chou Tian] held her in her arms. [Xiyan Wang] was pale with shock. Looking around, you saw the small window was still open from the inside, the brazier had tipped over by the wall, and there was an empty bowl on the table by the bed.

Your Story
Your name is [Sunian Yu]. You were born in the 20th year of Emperor Guangxu’s reign (1894). From as early as you can remember, your older sister [Madam Yu] took you to live in the Cui family manor—you know nothing about your parents, only remembering your sister as your sole family member. Your sister wanted to hide the family feud from you, but in the end, you guessed the enemy and planned to take revenge.
When you were two years old (1895), your sister got married. Her husband [Mengdang Zhou] (your brother-in-law) brought with him his younger brother, [Chitong Zhou], who was eight years older than you, to live in the front courtyard. You later heard that your sister was once a maid to Madam [Madam Wang], and that her marriage was arranged by the master, [Shouheng Cui].
Shortly after marrying, your sister brought young you to the Cui manor to live with her and her new family—she and your brother-in-law lived in the inner room, while [Chitong Zhou] took care of you in the outer room.
A year later (1896), unwilling to have his family live under someone else’s roof, your brother-in-law decisively enlisted in the army, saying he wanted to secure a future for the children, and asked [Chitong Zhou] to look after the family for him. When he left the Cui manor, your sister was already pregnant. Fortunately, [Shouheng Cui] still allowed you to stay in the manor, and Madam sometimes gave your sister financial help.
When your sister was about to give birth, you were too young to understand anything, only able to cry at her side. Thankfully, Madam sent for a midwife who helped your sister deliver a healthy baby boy—both mother and child were safe. Since your sister couldn’t read, she asked Madam to name the baby, who was given the name [Lianyi Zhou]. You now had a nephew.
At that time, Madam was also pregnant. After your sister recovered from childbirth, she moved with [Lianyi Zhou] back to the north courtyard, living in the west room, taking care of both her child and Madam, and earning some household money at the same time. [Chitong Zhou] continued to take care of you in the front courtyard, helping your sister by looking after you. Not wanting to be mocked for being idle, [Chitong Zhou] worked in the kitchen, making a little money, and always kept you close for safety. Once you grew up a bit and could help carry firewood, he didn’t have to watch you every moment.
In early winter, Madam gave birth to a daughter, who was named [Yannan Cui].
For the next few years, your brother-in-law came back to the Cui manor every year to visit, but he never got promoted and would leave after only a few days. You were still very young, sometimes going to the north courtyard where the women lived to find your sister, playing together with [Lianyi Zhou] and [Yannan Cui] without worrying about gender separation—your childhoods were intertwined.
At age 7 (1900), you saw that [Yannan Cui] had both her parents and asked your sister about your own parents. Your sister hurriedly took you outside the north courtyard—you saw her eyes turn red but didn’t stop asking. Eventually, your sister led you out of the manor to a deserted grave, had you kneel, and told you to kowtow to the deceased inside, calling him “father.”
Obeying your sister, you knelt and kowtowed. Unable to read the wooden tombstone, you again asked your sister how your father died.
Your sister was silent for a long time before finally saying that creditors had sent people who forced him to his death. No matter how much you begged after that, your sister refused to tell you who was responsible for your father’s death. She finally broke down in tears, saying that she and you were now living in the home of your enemy, and that telling you would do no good—if you kept pushing her, she’d rather not live anymore.
At that point, you had already guessed that the creditor who drove your father to his death was [Shouheng Cui], who had long been a moneylender, so you stopped asking.
The next evening, your sister brought [Lianyi Zhou] to where you and [Chitong Zhou] lived, and asked if [Chitong Zhou] was in. You said he wasn’t—he had just gone out to the courtyard.
Your sister finally let out a sigh of relief, told you to keep the grave visit a secret, and then made you swear to always take care of [Lianyi Zhou], no matter what happened. Though uneasy, you nodded and agreed.
By the time [Chitong Zhou] came back, your sister had already returned to the north courtyard with the sleeping [Lianyi Zhou]. [Chitong Zhou] asked why your sister had visited. Wanting to keep the secret, you said she had just come with the child to see him, didn’t say much, and left before seeing him. After that, you never saw your sister again.
After your sister disappeared, Madam had [Chitong Zhou] bring [Lianyi Zhou] out of the north courtyard (where the women lived) to stay with you two. Unable to find his mother, [Lianyi Zhou] cried every day, and remembering your promise to your sister, you always tried to comfort him. When [Lianyi Zhou] asked if his mother had come back, you would run to the north courtyard to check, hoping in your heart your sister would reappear, but there was never any news.
Not long after, your brother-in-law returned to visit, but when he didn’t see your sister, he left to drink and only returned home drunk, needing [Chitong Zhou] to help him to bed, then soon left the manor again.
At the end of autumn, you went again to the north courtyard to ask about your sister. Hearing shouting from [Xiyan Wang]’s room, you ran over. Maid [Aunt He] saw you and told you to quickly fetch saltwater from the kitchen to help [Xiyan Wang], who had tried to kill herself with rat poison, vomit. Madam soon arrived, and after hearing [Aunt He] explain, found out that [Xiyan Wang] had attempted suicide. By then, you had already formed the idea of avenging your father, so when you heard about the rat poison, you took advantage of the chaos to pocket most of the leftover poison, dumping the rest and its wrapper back on the ground. Later, [Shouheng Cui] brought a doctor, picked up the wrapper, showed it to the doctor, and then threw it away.
The failed suicide [Xiyan Wang] was Madam’s younger sister. Afraid she’d try again, Madam stayed with her, and by winter, [Xiyan Wang] became [Shouheng Cui]’s concubine, the two sisters now serving the same husband.
When you helped [Lianyi Zhou] go to the north courtyard to ask if your sister had come back, you saw [Xiyan Wang] arguing with Madam, which scared [Yannan Cui]. You took [Yannan Cui] out to play in the front courtyard with [Lianyi Zhou]. [Yannan Cui] said she’d seen her father go to [Xiyan Wang]’s room looking for her aunt and that he hadn’t returned home that night. She wanted to go fetch him, but her mother wouldn’t allow it, sighing as she sent [Gan Bao] to take her to the south courtyard for the night.
When you were eight (1901), [Shouheng Cui] discovered money missing from the household and searched everyone’s rooms in the front courtyard but couldn’t find it, so he suspected Madam had stolen it. Madam was so angered she fell ill that night. You already knew [Shouheng Cui] was a bad person, but with little rat poison left and only one chance, you wrapped it in oiled paper, kept it with you, and waited patiently.
That winter, when you delivered firewood to the kitchen, you saw [Shouheng Cui] order two bowls of red bean rice wine, one for himself and one for [Xiyan Wang]—the latter’s bowl was to have extra rock sugar. When the cook finished the two bowls, adding more sugar to one as instructed, you saw your chance. While no one was looking, you put the rat poison in [Shouheng Cui]’s bowl, planning to poison him. You burned the poison’s oiled wrapper in the stove. When [Shouheng Cui] picked up both bowls, he even asked which had extra sugar. Unexpectedly, maid [Gan Bao] took the poisoned bowl back to the kitchen to add more sugar and brought it to Madam to drink.
When you heard that Madam had died coughing blood, [Shouheng Cui] suspected the doctor had given the wrong medicine and accused him at the authorities, forcing the doctor to pay a large sum for funeral expenses. You always thought Madam was a good person and grieved her death. You later heard that Madam’s face was blackened after death (you had poisoned her, failing to avenge your father and losing your only chance, filling you with both hatred and regret). That year, you didn’t see your brother-in-law return to the manor and knew nothing of his situation.
After the new year (1902), [Xiyan Wang] was made the new Madam.
At the end of summer, your brother-in-law finally became a deputy military officer and returned to the manor to take away [Chitong Zhou] and [Lianyi Zhou]. Realizing you could no longer seek revenge and not wanting to live with your enemies, you knelt before your brother-in-law, begging to go with them. [Chitong Zhou] and [Lianyi Zhou], having shared joys and hardships with you all these years, also begged for you, so your brother-in-law agreed to take you along.
You remember that it rained the day you left the manor, with only [Yannan Cui] coming out to see you off. After leaving, you first stayed with your brother-in-law in the military camp between the two Huai rivers, watching him drill soldiers every day. Later, you and [Lianyi Zhou] were sent to school, and [Chitong Zhou] found a job at a foreign firm.
In the first year of the Republic of China (1912), men cut off their queues and strict gender segregation ended. Your brother-in-law, formerly in the Qing army, now served in the new army, with his superior being [Sichong Ni], a trusted official of the president.
Last year (1913), you applied to teach at a new-style school in Hefei city, becoming known as Mr. Yu.
This year (1914), warlords all over the country amassed their own armies. Your brother-in-law was transferred to Hefei, and [Lianyi Zhou] came to the school to tell you. There, he saw a female student, walked over and called her [Yannan Cui]. Surprised to be recognized, [Yannan Cui] nodded. [Lianyi Zhou], excited, gave his name and said he had just returned to Hefei and would visit [Yannan Cui]’s home once he was settled. Childhood friends, reunited suddenly, naturally had much to say, but before they could talk, the bell rang, and [Yannan Cui] had to return to class. [Lianyi Zhou] then left the school to rejoin your brother-in-law.
You didn’t want to reveal your identity to the daughter of your enemy, but unexpectedly [Yannan Cui] recognized you when she came to ask about [Lianyi Zhou]. She was so moved she almost cried. [Yannan Cui] said she had always remembered you and [Lianyi Zhou], and had never forgotten your days playing together as children—those were her happiest times. Though you knew [Yannan Cui] was [Shouheng Cui]’s daughter, you couldn’t help reminiscing with her about those childhood days.
[Yannan Cui] came to see you every day after that, and your relationship soon surpassed that of childhood friends. [Yannan Cui] knew you were a teacher but fell in love with you anyway. You never let go of your desire for revenge, knowing you would one day kill [Shouheng Cui], but couldn’t control your feelings and fell rapidly in love with [Yannan Cui].
You and [Yannan Cui] kept your romance secret from the school, maintaining the appearance of teacher and student in public. Only at night, outside school under the stars, could you meet openly.
In September, the school had to close because its funding was used for other purposes and the students returned home. After getting the school’s notice, you didn’t go to see [Yannan Cui]. You thought you would gradually forget her, but instead, you missed her even more. At the same time, you dreamed many times of your sister and the old grave, swearing again and again never to forget your father’s vengeance for the sake of love. To prove it, you must kill [Shouheng Cui].
During the Mid-Autumn Festival (October 4), [Chitong Zhou] invited you to drink, saying that after your brother-in-law’s superior was promoted to General, the governor’s mansion was now called the General’s Office and was going to be wired for electric lights—a chance to make money. [Chitong Zhou] only knew that electric bulbs (incandescent lamps) were invented decades ago and light up when connected to power. You told [Chitong Zhou] that lamp filaments had changed from carbon to tungsten, with the latest spiral tungsten filament invented last year (1913) by an American.
While explaining, you realized this was your opportunity for revenge! You’d dreamed up several plans for revenge over the years, and now could finally carry one out. You first suggested a big-money idea to [Chitong Zhou]—instead of letting foreigners get the profits, why not start your own factory? With your brother-in-law’s army connections, you could open doors at the General’s Office, earn a big sum from their lighting purchases, and later sell all over the province and the country.
[Chitong Zhou] said it was a good idea but wondered where to find the capital for a factory.
You had already thought it through but first pretended to discuss with [Chitong Zhou], then smoothly brought up [Shouheng Cui]—now that your brother-in-law was in Hefei, he could use his military background to approach [Shouheng Cui], offer a share of the profits, and by mixing threat with reward, definitely get him to invest!
After talking things over with [Chitong Zhou] in the morning, you went to prepare the demonstration equipment, while he went to persuade your brother-in-law.
On October 13, you finished preparing the equipment and your notes, and went to see your brother-in-law—he had heard everything from [Chitong Zhou], and, not knowing much about foreign goods, left all the details to you, only asking to save money where possible. So, you had him use his military connections to requisition a three-level painted barge you had picked out, to invite [Shouheng Cui] aboard.
On October 15, at noon, your brother-in-law, in civilian clothes, took you and [Lianyi Zhou] on the barge across the lake, docking at the big pier by the shore and preparing a banquet. In the afternoon, [Shouheng Cui] and [Chitong Zhou] arrived by carriage and were welcomed onto the boat. You had already measured and calculated the distances on the boat (as part of your plan for revenge), and instructed the boatmen to moor it exactly as you specified and drop anchor.
You took the generator from its case, assembled it, and called [Lianyi Zhou] to help you fix it to the front deck of the barge. Then you pulled out two long wires, one running up to a tree on the west side of the pier, where you strung several glass bulbs, ready to turn on the electricity and demonstrate after dark.
After you and [Lianyi Zhou] finished hanging the glass bulbs and checking the wiring under the tree, [Chitong Zhou] got off the boat to ask what you were doing. Before you could answer, you saw [Yannan Cui] approaching with two women. Seeing them, [Chitong Zhou] took you and [Lianyi Zhou] over to greet them.
Act II:
After the greetings
[Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] both accepted [Chitong Zhou]'s invitation, and [Xiyan Wang] also nodded in agreement. Together, you boarded the pleasure boat, carefully avoiding the generator, electric wires, and tools installed at the front of the boat, and walked into the banquet hall to meet your brother-in-law and [Shouheng Cui], who were already drinking inside. Your brother-in-law stood up to welcome you, [Chitong Zhou] suggested watching the incandescent lights together after dark, and then you all reminisced about the past at Cuizhuang...
You are about to kill [Shouheng Cui], so you dare not look at [Yannan Cui] and deliberately avoid her gaze.
After all, there is a difference between men and women. [Xiyan Wang] then took [Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] to a small banquet room on the second floor for a drink, leaving the men in the main banquet hall—[Chitong Zhou] tried to persuade [Shouheng Cui] to invest and, together with [Lianyi Zhou], kept toasting him. You also tried to get him drunk, talking about the factory's prospects and pouring more wine for [Shouheng Cui]—under your collective persuasion, [Shouheng Cui], who was already a bit tipsy, drank even more.
When night fell, everyone stepped out of the cabin and stood on the south side deck of the first floor of the boat. The cool breeze sobered you all up a bit. You went to crank the hand generator, the current quickly connected to the small electric lamp, emitting a flash of light—it was not dazzling, more like a faint glow of rotting grass, unable to outshine the bright moon.
[Chou Tian] was unsteady on her feet from the sudden light, and [Yannan Cui] caught her just in time—[Chou Tian] said she felt unwell from the wind, so [Yannan Cui] helped her back to the cabin, with [Xiyan Wang] and [Chitong Zhou] following inside.
You stopped cranking, and saw your brother-in-law whispering to [Shouheng Cui], inviting him to return to the cabin to continue drinking, with [Lianyi Zhou] keeping them company. You then heard [Shouheng Cui] laugh heartily, saying something about meeting his future in-laws, and calling for [Yannan Cui] to join. You never intended to showcase the electric lamp’s brightness, so you chose the simplest, easiest-to-disassemble setup. After pretending to be dissatisfied with the results and inspecting the equipment, you secretly removed the magnet with a tool and hid it on yourself. (The generator was stolen from your school, originally sealed in a box and tied with a rope. During the day, you instructed the boatmen not to set sail once you connected the wires, before dismantling the equipment. You also bluffed the boatmen that the machine was rented from a foreign firm and if the boat moved suddenly, dragging the machine into the river, they wouldn’t be able to pay for it!)
Later, you saw [Yannan Cui] planning to take [Shouheng Cui] home, and [Lianyi Zhou] went to help. Your brother-in-law asked [Chitong Zhou] to take a lantern and escort them. After seeing the four of them leave the boat, you told your brother-in-law that you had drunk too much and wanted to go back to your room to rest, and would wait until morning to dismantle the equipment—you knew your brother-in-law always kept a crossbow and arrows by his side, but tonight, due to the banquet, he left them in Room C on the second floor where he and [Lianyi Zhou] were staying.
You went up to the second floor, first passing by the small banquet room, where you saw [Chou Tian] slumped over the table, apparently drunk, with [Xiyan Wang] by her side talking to her. You then went to Room C, took your brother-in-law's crossbow, returned to Room B to get a vest stuffed with cotton and the long rope used for tying boxes, then hurried up to the third floor and entered the observation room.
You knew long ago that after Mid-Autumn, [Shouheng Cui] would keep an iron brazier in his room, burning charcoal at night for warmth. To avoid carbon monoxide poisoning, he would open the small window in the master room for ventilation—the brazier was always placed in a fixed position, and you used this to arrange the boat's docking spot—the master room floor was about 40 centimeters high, and from the outside, the small window was more than 2 meters above the ground, higher than the courtyard wall, and the third floor of the boat was higher than the small window.
You opened the south-facing window of the observation room and waited for a while. When you saw the north-side small window of the master room open and light shining from inside, you didn’t dare light the charcoal stove in the observation room for fear of being seen, so you wore the vest to keep warm, watching outside. When you confirmed that only the charcoal fire was left in the master room, you knew your enemy was alone—[Xiyan Wang] didn’t live in the master room, and you just saw [Chou Tian] in the small banquet room, so there was no risk of killing the wrong person!
You found the right angle by the window, aimed below the brazier, and shot the magnet tied with a rope toward it.
The window was not close. You missed twice, the magnet hitting the wall and making two noises each time. You pulled the magnet back with the rope and tried again, finally hitting the brazier on the third shot with a clang—the magnet stuck to it. You slowly pulled the rope, dragging the brazier to the wall, then kept pulling until the brazier was lifted up and the charcoal spilled onto the blue brick floor, still burning—you pulled the brazier to the window, blocking half the window for ventilation. After a while, the sleeping [Shouheng Cui] would die of carbon monoxide poisoning.
You pulled the rope tight, tied it to a hook in the observation room used for hanging signs, went downstairs to return the crossbow to Room C, and felt slightly relieved when you came out. You stood by the window in the corridor on the second floor and saw someone holding a lantern running to the main dock—it was [Yannan Cui]! You guessed [Yannan Cui] was coming to find you and quickly went downstairs. Seeing the banquet hall lit by candlelight and hearing vague conversations inside, you quietly left the cabin and met [Yannan Cui] on the first floor deck. You whispered in her ear that your brother-in-law was probably still in the banquet hall and told her not to alert him.
[Yannan Cui] lowered her voice and blurted out that she missed you. Afraid of being seen, you blew out the candle in [Yannan Cui]'s lantern, pulled her into the cabin, and instead of going upstairs, led her into the Room A across the hall.
You noticed [Yannan Cui] was not dressed warmly, so after entering the room, you immediately took off the vest over your robe and hugged her—[Yannan Cui] nestled against you, both of you feeling each other's warmth.
After a while, you heard a woman outside ask, "Madam, I’m going home now. Would you like to come with me?" Then you heard another woman's voice reply, "I still have some matters to discuss, you go ahead."
"Yes, madam." After the first woman finished, you heard someone leave the cabin.
[Yannan Cui] held you, unwilling to let go. You stroked her hair and softly asked about her life after leaving school and returning home. While [Yannan Cui] answered, you heard footsteps in the corridor walking westward.
By the time you coaxed [Yannan Cui] to sleep, it was already October 16th—you estimated [Shouheng Cui] was dead, and looking down at the sleeping [Yannan Cui], although you had never thought of using her for revenge, a trace of guilt flashed in your heart. You took off your vest and covered her with it, then went back upstairs to the observation room, pulled the magnet back with force, and the iron basin covering the small window fell with a bang—at that time, it was not yet dawn and there was no movement in the master room.
You untied the magnet, threw the rope from the north window of the observation room onto the box outside, but accidentally let the magnet stick to the lid of the charcoal stove in the room—you pried it off with effort, making no noise, then went downstairs to the first floor deck, and in the moonlight, reinstalled the magnet in the generator. To avoid [Yannan Cui], you didn’t return to Room A, but went upstairs to your own Room B to sleep.
After dawn, you were woken up by a knock at the door. When you opened it, you saw [Lianyi Zhou], who dragged you to the first-floor corridor without explanation. Then you saw [Chitong Zhou] and [Chou Tian] coming in from outside the cabin, [Yannan Cui] coming out of Room A, and [Xiyan Wang] coming down from the second floor—before you had time to ask what happened, [Lianyi Zhou] pointed to the banquet hall and shouted, "My father has been killed!"
Act III:
What happened next
At noon, after you had all seen your brother-in-law’s body, you stayed on the boat, keeping everything unchanged, just as you were about to discuss what conclusion to draw, Cuizhuang’s steward [Uncle Fu] ran into the cabin.
[Uncle Fu], panting, said he came to look for the madam, the concubine, and the young lady. After seeing them, he greeted them respectfully before speaking. At dawn, he saw [Lianyi Zhou] leave the north courtyard to bid him farewell, carrying the lantern borrowed the night before, and hurriedly left Cuizhuang, seemingly having said goodbye to the master—[Uncle Fu] recalled that the master came back drunk last night and did not dare disturb him immediately.
After it was fully bright, [Uncle Fu] still did not see the master get up, so he went to knock on the master room door, but no one answered.
[Uncle Fu] said he tried to push the door and found it bolted from inside, knowing that the master had the habit of locking the door before sleep. He knocked several more times and called out, but there was still no response. [Uncle Fu] then panicked, unable to find the madam, concubine, or young lady at home, and heard that they were all invited to the pleasure boat yesterday. Only the young lady accompanied the master home last night, but was not at Cuizhuang at the moment.
Not daring to make decisions on his own, [Uncle Fu] came here to ask the madam, concubine, and young lady to return home and take charge.
After listening, [Xiyan Wang] took you and [Yannan Cui] back home, with you, [Chitong Zhou], and [Lianyi Zhou] accompanying them, following [Uncle Fu] back to Cuizhuang. You entered the north courtyard and tried knocking on the master room door several more times, but there was still no response.
[Yannan Cui] became anxious and told [Uncle Fu] to quickly find someone to break down the door—but before [Uncle Fu] could call for the house servants, you, [Chitong Zhou], and [Lianyi Zhou] worked together to break open the door—once the bolt was broken, the door finally opened. You went inside and saw [Shouheng Cui] lying sideways on the bed, his face still showing a drunken flush but completely motionless. [Chitong Zhou] stepped forward to check, and hurriedly told everyone that [Shouheng Cui] was dead, his body already cold.
[Yannan Cui] immediately burst into tears, [Chou Tian] hugged her, [Xiyan Wang] was pale with shock—you glanced around the room and saw the small window was open from inside, the brazier was overturned by the wall, and there was an empty bowl on the table beside the bed.

Your Story
Your full name is [Xiyan Wang]. You are originally from Wuhu, Anhui Province, born in the eighth year of Emperor Guangxu’s reign (1882). Your ancestors once produced a successful imperial examination candidate, but unfortunately, later generations repeatedly failed the exams, and the family eventually declined. Afterward, your father ([Shunming Wang]) married off your elder sister Xirong (who is six years older than you) to [Shouheng Cui].
The year after your sister’s marriage (1894), both your parents passed away. With nowhere to turn, you wrote a letter to your sister and were brought to Cuizhuang. Among those who came to fetch you was a young man named [Mengdang Zhou], who accompanied you all the way from Wuhu to Cuizhuang. You had never left home before, and after stepping outside, you felt anxious and kept talking to [Mengdang Zhou] on the way to ease your worries.
After arriving at Cuizhuang, you were arranged to stay in the North Courtyard ([Xiyan Wang]’s room). Your brother-in-law ([Shouheng Cui]) assigned you a maid who was four years younger than you, named [Gan Bao], to serve you personally.
At that time, apart from your sister, [Mengdang Zhou] was the only person you knew in Cuizhuang. You often went to the front courtyard to talk to him and learned that his family had ties with your brother-in-law’s family. Later, like you, he lost both his parents and relied on others for support, bringing his younger brother [Chitong Zhou] along—[Mengdang Zhou] didn’t want to live off others for nothing, so with his martial skills, he helped your brother-in-law with tasks. At that time, you began to experience the stirrings of young love. Although you liked [Mengdang Zhou], you could only meet him in secret and didn’t dare let your sister or brother-in-law know.
When you were 14 (1895), you and [Mengdang Zhou] developed mutual feelings, but you didn’t know when you could truly be together. After Mid-Autumn Festival, the weather turned cold and your heart was full of sorrow—you hadn’t seen [Mengdang Zhou] for several days and felt frustrated. When [Gan Bao] brought you a basin of hot water and accidentally scalded you, you took the opportunity to scold her. Unexpectedly, the little maid suddenly burst into tears, making you even more upset, and you raised your hand to hit her.
Frightened, [Gan Bao] ran away. You chased her into the yard and ran into your sister’s maid [Liu Bao], who stopped you. Unable to hit [Gan Bao], you angrily threatened her, saying you would beat her to death when she came back—but [Gan Bao] didn’t return that night. You reported [Gan Bao]’s disappearance to the master’s room, and your brother-in-law reassured you that he would send someone to find her.
After some days, [Mengdang Zhou] brought [Gan Bao] back to you. Seeing [Gan Bao] dirty and thin in her old clothes, you guessed she must have suffered outside and didn’t scold her again.
Your brother-in-law then arranged for [Liu Bao] to marry [Mengdang Zhou]—by the time you learned of this, [Mengdang Zhou] and [Liu Bao] had already completed their wedding ceremony in Cuizhuang.
Not wanting to hear the noise of the wedding from the front courtyard, you covered your ears and hid in your room to cry—fortunately, [Gan Bao] had gone to watch the festivities, so she didn’t see you in such a sorry state.
After [Liu Bao] married [Mengdang Zhou], she moved to the front courtyard with her husband. You heard from the maid [Aunt He] that [Liu Bao] also brought her younger brother to live in Cuizhuang.
The next year (1896), [Mengdang Zhou] left his pregnant wife [Liu Bao] to join the army, leaving his family to be cared for by his brother [Chitong Zhou]. Later, [Liu Bao] gave birth to a boy. Unable to read, she asked your sister to name her son, who was named [Lianyi Zhou]. Your sister was also pregnant at the time. After her postpartum confinement, [Liu Bao] moved back to the North Courtyard with [Lianyi Zhou], living in the west room. She took care of her child and your sister while earning a little income—every day you would bring [Gan Bao] to visit your sister in the master’s room. Privately, you asked [Liu Bao] if there was any news of [Mengdang Zhou], but she only shook her head.
In early winter, your sister gave birth to a daughter, named [Yannan Cui]. At that time, you hadn’t yet noticed that your brother-in-law’s gaze toward you had changed...
In the years that followed, [Mengdang Zhou] returned to Cuizhuang every year to visit family. You continued to meet him secretly, hiding it from [Liu Bao]. After parting, you missed him even more, which led you to resent [Liu Bao] and find fault with her, scolding her at any opportunity.
When you were 18 (1899), your sister wanted you to get married, but you couldn’t forget [Mengdang Zhou] and refused.
A year later (1900), [Liu Bao] disappeared. Your sister had [Chitong Zhou] take [Lianyi Zhou] away from the North Courtyard, where the women lived, to stay with him and [Liu Bao]’s brother.
When [Mengdang Zhou] returned to visit family and didn’t see his wife, he went to the riverside outside the village to drink away his sorrow. Hearing this, you went out to find him and stayed with him until dusk. He promised you that when he became an officer, he would take you and his family away from Cuizhuang—if [Liu Bao] still hadn’t been found by then, he would marry you!
After Mid-Autumn, your sister became unwell and borrowed [Gan Bao] to help her take care of [Yannan Cui] in the master’s room—you were just waiting for [Mengdang Zhou] to fulfill his promise, but also worried that [Liu Bao] might return. You even had nightmares about [Liu Bao] appearing when [Mengdang Zhou] married you.
In late autumn, your brother-in-law announced that he wanted to take you as a concubine. You were shocked, first thinking of escaping, but with nowhere to go, you resolved to commit suicide—you had the maid [Aunt He] buy a packet of rat poison, took some, and soon collapsed in pain, losing consciousness. [Aunt He] called for help, salt water was given to induce vomiting, and your brother-in-law summoned a doctor, who managed to save you—when you barely opened your eyes, you saw your brother-in-law pick up the packet of poison.
Your sister, afraid you might try again, watched over you constantly until winter. Then she personally dressed you in red and sent you to the bridal chamber, from then on sharing a husband with her. Because of this, you hated your sister, fought with her whenever you met, and insisted you didn’t want to live together. [Shouheng Cui] allowed you to stay in your original room ([Xiyan Wang]’s room) and came to visit you at night.
When you were 20 (1901), your sister kept [Gan Bao] with her, and [Shouheng Cui] planned to buy you a new maid. When you went to the master’s room to get silver, you found that money was missing from the locked box under the bed.
[Shouheng Cui] immediately suspected a thief in the house, searched everyone’s quarters in the front courtyard, but found nothing and then suspected your sister had taken it. Angry at the accusation, your sister fell ill that night—you were still resentful and didn’t visit her, only knowing that the doctor said she had caught a chill. She moved with [Gan Bao] and [Yannan Cui] to the west room, and did not survive the winter.
You still remember the day your sister died, you wanted to eat sweet rice wine from your hometown and told [Shouheng Cui]. He had the kitchen cook it, brought it to you himself, and you both had a bowl, but [Gan Bao] took one of the bowls. Not long after you finished your bowl, you heard that your sister’s condition had suddenly worsened. She died of coughing up blood before the doctor could arrive. Before dying, she said to leave all her jewelry to you.
Only after losing your sister did you regret not being by her side when she needed you most. Later, seeing her corpse’s darkened face, you became even more suspicious. [Shouheng Cui] suspected the doctor had given the wrong medicine, reported it to the magistrate, and won a large compensation for funeral expenses from the medical clinic.
The following year (1902), [Shouheng Cui] made you his principal wife, and you felt even guiltier toward your sister. You refused to move to the master’s room, instead staying where you were with your new maid ([Gui Bao]), sometimes dreaming of your sister and apologizing to her in your dreams.
What pained you even more was that soon after, [Mengdang Zhou] became an officer as promised, but still could not marry you. At the end of summer, [Mengdang Zhou] planned to take away his brother and son, as well as [Liu Bao]’s brother, but didn’t see you—when you learned of all this, you could only hide and cry once again, helplessly accepting your fate.
Three years later (1904), [Shouheng Cui] made [Gan Bao] his concubine, moving her from the west room to the master’s room. [Gan Bao] then changed her name to [Chou Tian]. [Yannan Cui] did not want [Shouheng Cui] to take another concubine and went to her father to argue—[Shouheng Cui] was furious and scolded [Yannan Cui], who had lost her mother, making her move out of the North Courtyard to the South Courtyard to be cared for by a senior maid ([Yang Bao]).
For years you heard nothing of [Mengdang Zhou], and resigned yourself to your fate, living as the wife in Cuizhuang, watching the seasons pass, letting your youth fall away with the leaves, never interfering in your husband’s affairs. When bored, you would go to the South Courtyard to see [Yannan Cui], teaching her to read and spending time together. At night, you would stay to keep [Yannan Cui] company, sometimes seeing [Chou Tian] visiting her in the South Courtyard.
In the first year of the Republic of China (1912), men cut off their queues, and strict separation of men and women was no longer enforced.
Last year (1913), [Chou Tian] pleaded for [Yannan Cui] in front of [Shouheng Cui], and persuaded him to let [Yannan Cui] attend a modern school in Hefei and board there. After that, [Yannan Cui] only came home on holidays, and the senior maid who used to take care of her moved to the west room of the North Courtyard to continue serving the women.
Since taking [Chou Tian] as a concubine, [Shouheng Cui] rarely came to your room, and you no longer went to the master’s room to see him. Now, even when you stand under the Mid-Autumn moon and feel the cool breeze, you have no more sorrow in your heart—you thought the rest of your life would be as still as a dry well, but did not expect your heart to be stirred again.
This year (1914), in September, [Mengdang Zhou] returned to Hefei and asked [Aunt He] to arrange a meeting with you outside Cuizhuang, in the woods west of the bridge.
You heard from [Mengdang Zhou] that he had not remarried all these years, and for a moment you were so moved you almost wanted to cry in his arms—but your husband [Shouheng Cui] is a well-known local wealthy man, and your remarriage was completely legitimate. Whatever you and [Mengdang Zhou] did would violate propriety. No matter how much resentment you held, you could only ask [Mengdang Zhou] why he hadn’t come back sooner, and why he only appeared after you had married [Shouheng Cui].
[Mengdang Zhou] sighed deeply and remained silent. You dared not face your former lover any longer, turned and ran back to Cuizhuang in tears, unable to sleep all night, almost vowing never to see [Mengdang Zhou] again in this life. At dawn, you hurriedly took a valuable piece of jewelry from your chest and gave it to [Aunt He], asking her to promise not to reveal that [Mengdang Zhou] had asked to meet you.
A few days later, [Yannan Cui] suddenly returned home and never went back to school, staying in Cuizhuang.
At Mid-Autumn (October 4), the whole family celebrated together. The full moon still shone in the sky, but mortal hearts remained unfulfilled.
On October 12, you saw a somewhat familiar man talking with [Shouheng Cui] in the guest room for a long time. Afterwards, [Shouheng Cui] let him stay, and upon inquiry you learned he was [Chitong Zhou]. Ever since [Mengdang Zhou]’s family left, the house they lived in had been used for storage, so [Shouheng Cui] let [Chitong Zhou] stay in a guest room in the front courtyard.
On October 14, you heard that a young man had come to Cuizhuang to deliver invitations.
On October 15, in the afternoon, [Shouheng Cui] and [Chitong Zhou] went out by carriage to attend the event. [Yannan Cui] pulled [Chou Tian] along to chat with you. During this time, you heard from a maid that a large pleasure boat had arrived at the dock, and that the master had gone aboard. [Chou Tian] wanted to join the fun, and [Yannan Cui] pleaded with you, as the lady of the house, to take them out.
You nodded in agreement, and after getting ready together, you all set out, circling around Cuizhuang and heading toward the lake. By the big dock, you saw a three-story pleasure boat. From the lower deck, two long electric wires ran all the way to a tree on the west side of the dock, connected to several glass lamps.
[Chitong Zhou], dressed in a Western suit, stood under the tree with two young men. When they saw you, they came over to greet you.
Act II:
The Story After the Greetings
You notice that [Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] have both accepted [Chitong Zhou]’s invitation, and you also want to take this opportunity to see [Mengdang Zhou], so you pretend to act casually, nod in agreement, and follow [Chitong Zhou] and the others onto the pleasure boat. You carefully avoid the generator, wires, and tools installed at the front of the boat, walk into the banquet hall, and meet [Shouheng Cui] and [Mengdang Zhou], who are already inside drinking.
[Mengdang Zhou], dressed in casual clothes, stands up to welcome you. [Chitong Zhou] says that you’ll watch the incandescent lamp together after night falls, then starts reminiscing about the old days in Cuizhuang. You don’t want to face [Shouheng Cui] and [Mengdang Zhou] at the same time, so you deliberately lower your head.
After all, there is a difference between men and women, so you later take [Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] to the small banquet room on the second floor for a drink, while the men stay in the main hall. After drinking in the small banquet room, your mood changes; you even feel lucky that you never swore never to see [Mengdang Zhou] again, and memories of your passionate romance come flooding back—[Mengdang Zhou] once said he was skilled with a crossbow, then took out an arrow and swore to you that if he ever betrayed you, he would die by his own arrow! Hearing this, your heart melted, you snatched the arrow away and hid it, telling him not to say such things, and you thought [Mengdang Zhou] would soon come to propose to you.
When night falls, everyone steps out of the cabin and stands on the south deck of the first floor of the boat. The cool breeze sobers you up a bit—[Sunian Yu] turns the hand-cranked generator, and current quickly flows to the small lamps, producing a brief flash of light—they are not dazzling, more like glowworms among rotten grass, unable to compete with the bright moon.
[Chou Tian], dazzled by the light, stumbles, and [Yannan Cui] quickly supports her. [Chou Tian] says she feels unwell from the wind, so [Yannan Cui] helps her back to the cabin—you want to see if [Chou Tian] is drunk and feeling worse from the wind, so you follow them inside.
At this moment, [Sunian Yu] stops cranking the generator. You follow [Yannan Cui] and [Chou Tian] upstairs, with [Chitong Zhou] behind you, entering the small banquet room. After seeing [Yannan Cui] help [Chou Tian] to sit down, [Chou Tian] slumps over the table. Soon after, you hear a drunken male voice downstairs calling for [Yannan Cui], sounding like [Shouheng Cui].
You recall a few nights ago, when [Shouheng Cui] came to see you and said that [Yannan Cui] was of age to marry and this matter must be decided by him. You suspect [Shouheng Cui] already has someone in mind, though he didn’t say who, and you take [Yannan Cui] out of the small banquet room to ask if she likes anyone at the moment.
[Yannan Cui] immediately shakes her head, which reminds you how you were once forced into marriage by [Shouheng Cui] and your elder sister. You can’t help but sigh, telling [Yannan Cui] to go see what her father wants while you stay to look after [Chou Tian] until she sobers up.
In fact, you want to discuss [Yannan Cui]’s marriage with [Chou Tian] to hear her thoughts, so you return to the small banquet room, running into [Chitong Zhou] coming out—you wait for [Chitong Zhou] to leave, then go to wake up [Chou Tian], asking if she feels better now.
After mumbling drunkenly for a bit, [Chou Tian] slumps back down with her eyes closed—you realize she won’t sober up any time soon and can only leave the small banquet room helplessly.
You go downstairs, thinking that [Mengdang Zhou] is still in the banquet hall, and your heart starts racing—you know you shouldn’t go in, but you can’t help but push open the door to the banquet hall and see two singing girls attending to [Mengdang Zhou].
[Mengdang Zhou] sees you come in and immediately changes his expression, explaining that he just let them in to change the candles. The two singing girls, hearing [Mengdang Zhou]’s words, replace the candles and leave the banquet hall, gently closing the door behind them.
Now, there is no one else in the hall. You and [Mengdang Zhou] gaze at each other in the flickering candlelight, as if you’ve gone back to long ago. [Mengdang Zhou] once again says that he hasn’t married all these years—not because he’s waiting for [Liu Bao] to return, but because you are the only one in his heart!
You ask [Mengdang Zhou] to stop—now you are [Shouheng Cui]’s wife, and as long as your husband is alive, nothing is possible.
[Mengdang Zhou] has no choice but to change the subject, saying that he plans for his son to marry [Yannan Cui]. You also need to calm down, so you and [Mengdang Zhou] start discussing the possible marriage between [Yannan Cui] and [Lianyi Zhou], wondering if their birth dates are compatible and saying you should consult someone. This topic finally helps you relax. Later, you hear [Chou Tian] outside the hall asking, “Madam, I’m going back now, are you coming with me?”
You realize [Chou Tian] must be sober by now and has come down to find you, probably wanting to go home—once again, knowing you shouldn’t stay alone, you use [Yannan Cui]’s marriage as a reasonable excuse—you haven’t finished discussing this matter, so you can’t go back yet!
With a reason to stay, you reply to [Chou Tian] outside the door, “I still have some things to discuss, you go ahead.”
“Yes, Madam,” [Chou Tian] responds and leaves without entering the hall.
[Mengdang Zhou] realizes you haven’t left with [Chou Tian] and guesses your feelings, coming over to hold you tightly. In [Mengdang Zhou]’s embrace, you are confused, but you can’t forget your identity, and your emotions go back and forth. Unable to accept this forbidden passion immediately, you push [Mengdang Zhou] away, begging him to give you some time to think. [Mengdang Zhou] sits down with a bitter smile and says he’ll wait for you here.
When you leave the banquet hall, you glance back at [Mengdang Zhou] in the candlelight before gently closing the door, then turn upstairs and go into the small banquet room again, drinking several glasses of old wine in a row—you want alcohol to give you the courage to cross the line, but you still hesitate. Since your alcohol tolerance is poor, drinking quickly only makes you feel dizzy—you can barely hold your glass, slumping on the table and sobbing, saying it’s all his ([Mengdang Zhou]’s) fault. Why did he leave back then, and why did he come back now? Everything before your eyes grows more and more blurry behind your tears.
It isn’t until you hear a knock on the corridor door that you open your eyes and realize it’s already morning, October 16th.
You compose yourself, hear footsteps in the corridor, and realize you spent the night away from home, wanting to return to Cuizhuang immediately.
You go downstairs and see [Chitong Zhou] and [Chou Tian] coming in from outside the cabin, [Yannan Cui] emerging from the main cabin, and [Lianyi Zhou] in the corridor pulling [Sunian Yu], pointing at the banquet hall and shouting: “My father has been killed!”

Act III:
What Happens Next
At noon, after you all see [Mengdang Zhou]’s body, you stay on the pleasure boat together, keeping everything as it was, trying to reach a conclusion, when Cuizhuang’s butler [Uncle Fu] rushes into the cabin.
[Uncle Fu], out of breath, says he’s come to find Madam, Aunt, and Miss. After greeting you, [Uncle Fu] explains that at dawn he saw [Lianyi Zhou] leaving the north yard to say goodbye, carrying the lantern he borrowed last night, and left Cuizhuang in a hurry, apparently having already said farewell to the master—[Uncle Fu] recalls the master returned drunk last night, so he didn’t dare disturb him.
When it was fully bright, [Uncle Fu] still hadn’t seen the master get up, so he went to knock on the master bedroom door but got no response.
[Uncle Fu] says he tried pushing the door and found it barred from the inside. Knowing the master had a habit of bolting the door before sleeping, he knocked harder and called out, but there was still no movement—[Uncle Fu] started to panic. Unable to find Madam, Aunt, and Miss at home, and having heard they were all invited onto the boat yesterday, with only Miss returning with the master at night but now missing from Cuizhuang,
[Uncle Fu] dared not act on his own, so he rushed here to find Madam, Aunt, and Miss for advice.
After hearing this, you lead [Chou Tian] and [Yannan Cui] home, with [Chitong Zhou], [Sunian Yu], and [Lianyi Zhou] accompanying you, following [Uncle Fu] back to Cuizhuang. You enter the north yard and try knocking on the master bedroom door several times again, but still get no response.
[Yannan Cui] becomes anxious and urges [Uncle Fu] to get someone to break down the door. [Chitong Zhou], [Sunian Yu], and [Lianyi Zhou] do not wait for [Uncle Fu] to fetch servants, but work together to break open the master bedroom door—with a crash, the bolt breaks, the door finally opens, and you all enter to find [Shouheng Cui] lying on his side on the bed, his face still flushed as if from drinking, but motionless. [Chitong Zhou] steps forward, feels with his hand, and hurriedly tells everyone that [Shouheng Cui] is dead—his body is already cold.
[Yannan Cui] immediately bursts into tears, [Chou Tian] holds her in her arms—you are terrified, look around the room, and see the small window open from the inside, the brazier overturned by the wall, and an empty bowl on the table beside the bed.

Your Story
Your full name is [Yannan Cui], born in the 22nd year of Emperor Guangxu’s reign (1896). Your mother, [Xirong Wang], came from Wuhu, Anhui. There had once been a scholar in her family, but unfortunately, her descendants repeatedly failed the imperial examinations. Later, the family fell on hard times, and the eldest daughter (your mother) was married to the master of the Cui Estate, [Shouheng Cui] (your father).
Your mother told you that before you were born, her family had only her younger sister [Xiyan Wang] left—so she asked your father to send someone to bring [Xiyan Wang] (your aunt) from their old home in Wuhu to the Cui Estate. Since then, your aunt lived in the North Courtyard ([Xiyan Wang]’s room), and your father assigned her a personal maid named [Gan Bao].
The year after your aunt arrived at the Cui Estate (1895), your father arranged for your mother’s maid [Madam Yu] to marry [Mengdang Zhou], who lived in the Front Courtyard. This newlywed couple lived together with [Mengdang Zhou]’s younger brother [Chitong Zhou], as well as [Madam Yu]’s younger brother [Sunian Yu].
In the year you were born (1896), [Mengdang Zhou] left his pregnant wife [Madam Yu] and went off to join the army, leaving his family in the care of his younger brother [Chitong Zhou]. Later, [Madam Yu] gave birth to a boy. Since she was illiterate, she asked your mother to name her son, who was named [Lianyi Zhou]. At that time, your mother was already pregnant with you. After her confinement, [Madam Yu] moved back to the North Courtyard with [Lianyi Zhou], living in the west room—raising her child, serving your mother, and also earning some income for the household.
In early winter, you were born and named [Yannan Cui].
When you were four (1899), your mother wanted to find a husband for your aunt and talked to your father about it. By then, you already had memories, and you could remember a lot whenever you heard the adults talk about the past (for example, things before your birth were learned from the adults’ stories). You and [Lianyi Zhou] grew up together in the North Courtyard.
Although [Sunian Yu] was [Lianyi Zhou]’s uncle, he was only two years older than him and often came to the North Courtyard to see his sister [Madam Yu]. The three of you children played together from a young age without worrying about the difference between boys and girls. [Madam Yu] was skillful with her hands and made you an embroidered sachet. Your mother put some things inside and joked that you should remember to look at it when you get married one day.
A year later (1900), one night you saw [Madam Yu] leave the North Courtyard, and after that, you never saw her again. [Lianyi Zhou] came to the main room to find his mother and was invited by your mother to eat. A few days later, your mother asked [Chitong Zhou] to take [Lianyi Zhou] out of the North Courtyard, where the women lived, and back to the Front Courtyard.
After the Mid-Autumn Festival, your mother felt unwell and borrowed your aunt’s maid [Gan Bao] to help take care of you in the main room. [Gan Bao] was ten years older than you, had learned to read from your mother, and was very kind to you. Later, you heard adults say that your aunt was so distraught in her room that she wanted to die—you didn’t know what had happened.
At the end of autumn, just as your mother was recovering, she went to stay with your aunt. After winter set in, you saw your mother helping your aunt change into red clothes, after which your aunt was brought into the main room to sleep with your father… Back then, you didn’t realize your aunt had become your father’s concubine; you only knew that she and your mother sometimes quarreled, and later your aunt moved back to her original room.
[Sunian Yu] and [Lianyi Zhou] both hoped [Madam Yu] would return. [Sunian Yu] often came to the North Courtyard to ask if his sister was back. When he saw you scared because of the quarrels between your aunt and mother, he would take you out to find [Lianyi Zhou] and play together in the Front Courtyard. You told [Sunian Yu] and [Lianyi Zhou] that you saw your father going to [Xiyan Wang]’s room to see your aunt and not returning to the main room at night. You wanted to go get your father back, but your mother wouldn’t allow it. She sighed and asked maid [Gan Bao] to hold you and spend the night in the South Courtyard.
The first time you saw the dried-up well in the South Courtyard, there was already a wooden board chained and locked over the mouth of the well. You always cried for your father, and [Gan Bao] held you tightly while your mother wept nearby.
When you were six (1901), you liked [Gan Bao] so much that you begged your mother to keep her. Your mother agreed. Your father planned to buy a new maid for your aunt and discovered some money missing from the locked chest under the bed in the main room when he went to get silver for this purchase. He became furious.
You remembered that the chest was always locked, and the key was usually kept in the main room. When your mother took you and [Gan Bao] to the South Courtyard at night, she would call maid [Aunt He] to lock the North Courtyard gate from the inside after they left, and never took the key for the chest outside.
Your father first suspected a thief in the house and searched everyone’s quarters in the Front Courtyard but didn’t find the missing silver. He then suspected your mother had taken it. Upset, your mother fell ill that very night. The doctor diagnosed her with chills. Fearing contagion, your father asked your mother to move to the west room when he heard her coughing. You knew your father was always afraid of catching cold and getting sick. Every year after Mid-Autumn, he would put an iron brazier in the room for warmth at night and open a small window for ventilation to avoid carbon monoxide poisoning.
Your father later had half of the small window in the main room nailed shut and said to your mother that not even a ten-year-old child could climb in from outside now—let’s see if any more silver disappears.
You didn’t want to leave your mother, so [Gan Bao] took you to live in the west room and cared for your mother every day, brewing her medicine. Even so, your mother didn’t survive the winter.
The day your mother died, [Gan Bao] mentioned your aunt had ordered red bean rice wine soup from the kitchen, and your mother said she wanted some too. While [Gan Bao] went out to get it, your mother told you that both she and your aunt loved this snack as children, then said she had wronged your aunt.
After [Gan Bao] brought back a bowl of red bean rice wine soup, your mother drank it and soon after, her condition worsened. She began coughing blood and said if she died, all her jewelry would go to your aunt. Before the doctor could arrive, your mother died, her face turning dark.
After losing your mother, you were heartbroken, but [Gan Bao] stayed by your side the whole time. Your father suspected the doctor had given the wrong medicine and sued him at the local yamen, making him pay a large sum for funeral expenses.
After New Year’s (1902), your aunt was made the official wife, bringing with her a new maid your father bought ([Gui Bao]) and continued living in her original quarters ([Xiyan Wang]’s room).
At the end of summer, [Lianyi Zhou]’s father [Mengdang Zhou] returned to the Cui Estate and took his family away.
You couldn’t forget the day [Sunian Yu] and [Lianyi Zhou] left the Cui Estate. It was raining outside, and since you no longer had your mother, you didn’t want to lose your friends too. You ran out, crying, saying you didn’t want them to leave. [Lianyi Zhou] had to go with his father but promised you he would come back one day!
Three years later (1904), your father took [Gan Bao] as a concubine and had her move from the west room to the main room. You couldn’t bear to see [Gan Bao] go, so you ran to your father and protested, forbidding him to take her away. Your father was furious. Not only did he scold you for being motherless, but he also had you move out of the North Courtyard to the South Courtyard, now to be cared for by a head maid ([Yang Bao]).
After becoming a concubine, [Gan Bao] changed her name to [Chou Tian] and still often came to the South Courtyard to visit you, only returning at night. Besides [Chou Tian], your aunt also came to the South Courtyard to teach you to read and keep you company overnight.
In the first year of the Republic of China (1912), men cut off their queues, and segregation between men and women was no longer strictly enforced.
Last year (1913), you told [Chou Tian] that you wanted to go to school, and she pleaded with your father on your behalf. Your father finally agreed to let you board at a modern school in Hefei City.
You finally left the Cui Estate, returning only during holidays or festivals. The head maid who originally cared for you moved to the west room in the North Courtyard to continue serving the women of the household.
This year (1914), the school hired a new teacher of Western studies, Mr. Yu. You found him familiar and secretly followed him, later seeing him talking to a young man.
The young man noticed you and came over, calling your name—you nodded in surprise, also feeling he looked familiar. The young man beamed and first told you he was [Lianyi Zhou], then said he had just returned to Hefei and would visit your home after settling down.
Reuniting with your childhood friend brought you great joy, and you had many things to say. But before you could chat much with [Lianyi Zhou], the school bell rang, and you had to return to class. After that, you didn’t see [Lianyi Zhou] again at school.
When you asked Mr. Yu about [Lianyi Zhou], you recognized him as [Sunian Yu]—you thought you would never see them again, but to your surprise, here they were, and you were so moved you almost cried, quickly telling him that you had always remembered [Sunian Yu] and [Lianyi Zhou] and never forgotten your time playing together as children—it was your happiest time. When reminiscing with [Sunian Yu], you remembered the rainy day they left and felt sad you hadn’t said goodbye. Now, at last, you had no more regrets.
After that, you went to see [Sunian Yu] every day, and your relationship soon grew beyond childhood friendship—you knew [Sunian Yu] was your teacher, but you fell in love with him, and your relationship quickly became passionate. Your romance had to be kept secret from the school authorities, so you always addressed each other as teacher and student in public. Only late at night would you secretly meet outside the school, freely talking under the stars.
In September, classes were suddenly suspended at the school, and all students were sent home. As a teacher, [Sunian Yu] stayed in the school dormitory, waiting for news, and you returned home to the Cui Estate.
On the Mid-Autumn Festival (October 4), after celebrating with your family, you returned alone with a lantern to the South Courtyard. An autumn wind blew by, and you shivered, feeling even more melancholy thinking of when you would see [Sunian Yu] again.
On October 12, a visitor came to your house and talked with your father in the parlor for a long time. Afterward, he stayed as a guest in the Front Courtyard.
On October 14, you saw [Lianyi Zhou] come to the Cui Estate to deliver an invitation. You intended to see him after he met with your father, but he left after delivering the invitation.
On October 15, in the afternoon, your father and the guest drove out together to keep an appointment.
You missed school life and [Sunian Yu] even more. Feeling bored at home, you went to the main room to find [Chou Tian] and pulled her along to your aunt’s place for a chat. During your visit, a maid said a large pleasure boat had arrived at the wharf and that the master had gone aboard. [Chou Tian] wanted to see the excitement—you knew your aunt and [Chou Tian] had spent nearly their whole lives at the Cui Estate and always thought they should go out, so you begged your aunt, now the official wife, to take you all out.
Your aunt agreed. After dressing up, the three of you went out together, made your way around the Cui Estate, and walked to the lake. By the large pier, you saw a three-story pleasure boat. Two long electric wires stretched from the first deck to the trees on the west side of the dock, connected to several glass items (small bulbs).
You had seen these things at school and recognized them as light bulbs. You remembered your classes, where you learned that these were called incandescent bulbs, invented by Westerners only decades ago, with the filament changing from carbon to tungsten, glowing when powered. As you were thinking of what [Sunian Yu] had taught in class, you saw him standing under the tree!
You almost called out in excitement, then noticed that standing beside [Sunian Yu] were [Lianyi Zhou] and a man in a Western suit. When they saw you, they came over to greet you.

Act Two:
After the Small Talk
You and [Chou Tian] both accepted [Chitong Zhou]’s invitation, and your aunt also nodded in agreement. Together, you followed [Chitong Zhou] and the others onto the pleasure boat, carefully avoiding the generator, wires, and tools installed at the front, and entered the banquet hall, where you met your father and [Mengdang Zhou] who were drinking—[Mengdang Zhou], dressed in plain clothes, stood up to greet you. [Chitong Zhou] said you’d all watch the incandescent lights after dark, and then started reminiscing about old times in Cuizhuang. You and [Sunian Yu], afraid others might notice your relationship, deliberately avoided eye contact.
Since men and women should keep a proper distance, your aunt took you and [Chou Tian] to the small banquet room on the second floor for a few drinks, while the men stayed in the main hall. After being apart from [Sunian Yu] for some time, you finally met again, but had little chance to talk, which left you feeling down, so you drank a few extra cups of aged wine.
When night fell, everyone stepped out of the cabin and stood on the south deck of the boat. The cool breeze sobered you up a bit. [Sunian Yu] went to crank the hand generator, quickly sending power to the small electric lamps, which flashed briefly—not dazzling, but like fireflies among rotting grass, unable to compete with the bright moon.
[Chou Tian], dazzled by the lights, stumbled, and you caught her just in time. [Chou Tian] said she felt unwell from the wind. Not knowing when your father would return home, and thinking of how [Chou Tian] had had a few drinks, you offered to accompany her upstairs to rest, then helped her into the cabin.
At this moment, [Sunian Yu] stopped cranking the generator, and your aunt and [Chitong Zhou] also went up to the second floor. You helped [Chou Tian] into the small banquet room, and as soon as she sat down, she laid her head on the table. Then you heard a drunken man’s voice calling you from downstairs—it sounded like your father.
Your aunt pulled you out of the small banquet room and suddenly asked if you had someone you liked.
Startled, you immediately shook your head. Your aunt sighed, told you to go see what your father wanted, and said she’d stay to keep [Chou Tian] company until she sobered up.
After [Chitong Zhou] came out from the small banquet room, you walked downstairs with him behind you. You saw your father staggering, face flushed and smelling strongly of alcohol. Worried he’d get into trouble if he kept drinking, you decided to take him home. [Lianyi Zhou] came over to help. [Mengdang Zhou] told [Chitong Zhou] to fetch a lantern and escort your father, while [Sunian Yu] was inspecting the machinery and didn’t have time to say goodbye.
[Chitong Zhou] carried a lantern in one hand and, together with [Lianyi Zhou], supported your father. You followed them, carefully skirting the generator, wires, and tools at the front of the boat. There was no carriage, so you had to walk back to Cuizhuang.
Afraid your drunken father might catch a cold like your mother, you went to tell the servants to put burning charcoal into the brazier in the master bedroom after returning to Cuizhuang. [Lianyi Zhou] then helped your father into the north courtyard, and [Chitong Zhou] went to the kitchen in the front yard.
You watched the servant set up the brazier in the master bedroom. [Lianyi Zhou] supported your father as he sat on the bed, and [Chitong Zhou] brought in a bowl of water, which [Lianyi Zhou] took.
You went to open the small window in the master bedroom but couldn’t reach it. [Chitong Zhou] moved a chair from beside the bed, stood on it, opened half the window, and saw the deep night outside.
After [Chitong Zhou] stepped down and put the chair back, your father asked for water. You took the bowl from [Lianyi Zhou], handed it to your father, and watched as he finished it in one gulp.
Even after drinking the water, your father didn’t sober up and, regardless of [Chou Tian] not returning, said he wanted to rest after putting down the bowl. You all left the master bedroom and heard him bolt the door from inside.
You once heard from [Chou Tian] that, ever since your father lost his money, he had become extra cautious. He always kept the key to the lockbox with him and made sure to bolt the master bedroom door from the inside before sleeping—so much so that [Chou Tian] couldn’t get in if she came back late. You didn’t expect your father, even when drunk, not to forget this habit. He’d probably sleep until morning.
Seeing that it was already late, you let [Chitong Zhou] and [Lianyi Zhou] stay in the guest room.
After settling them in, you walked toward the south courtyard. At the gate, you suddenly recalled your aunt’s words and worried your father would force you to marry, so you planned to ask your aunt while your father was still drunk—since she hadn’t returned with you, you had to find a lantern, light a candle, and head back to the boat to find her.
Carrying the lantern, you carefully made your way past Cuizhuang to the big pier, boarded the pleasure boat, again maneuvered past the generator and tools, and before you even reached the cabin, saw [Sunian Yu] coming out to meet you on the deck.
You were overjoyed. [Sunian Yu] quietly told you that his brother-in-law was still in the banquet hall and not to alert him. Knowing [Sunian Yu] didn’t want your relationship discovered, but not wanting to hide your feelings either, you whispered that you missed him. [Sunian Yu] then blew out your candle and pulled you into the cabin. Seeing candlelight in the banquet hall and hearing conversation, you were afraid to be discovered, so you and [Sunian Yu] quietly entered the opposite cabin.
Having rushed over at night without extra clothes, you shivered a little. [Sunian Yu] immediately removed his vest and embraced you after entering the room. Leaning against him, you felt his warmth, and your face flushed even before your body warmed up.
After you warmed up a bit, you heard a woman outside say, “Madam, I’m going back now. Will you come with me?”—it sounded like [Chou Tian]. Then someone answered, “I have some things to discuss. You go back first,” which sounded like your aunt.
“All right, Madam.” After [Chou Tian] said this, you heard someone leave the boat.
Only then did you remember you had come to find your aunt, but unwilling to leave, you held [Sunian Yu] tightly. [Sunian Yu] stroked your head and whispered, seemingly asking about your life after leaving school and returning home. As you answered, you heard footsteps in the corridor heading west, and then relaxed, feeling warm and drowsy until you fell asleep.
You heard the sound of running in the corridor outside the cabin, opened your eyes, and realized it was morning—October 16th. You were still covered by [Sunian Yu]’s vest, feeling the warmth. Rubbing your eyes and turning your head, you didn’t see [Sunian Yu]. Startled, you stood up, put the vest on the table, and hearing footsteps coming downstairs, you left the cabin. You saw [Chitong Zhou] and [Chou Tian] coming in from outside, your aunt coming down from the second floor, and [Lianyi Zhou] in the corridor grabbing [Sunian Yu] and shouting toward the banquet hall: “My father has been murdered!”

Act Three:
The Story Continues
At noon, after you all saw [Mengdang Zhou]’s body and stayed on the pleasure boat to keep everything unchanged, just as you were about to discuss your conclusions, the Cuizhuang steward [Uncle Fu] rushed into the cabin.
[Uncle Fu], out of breath, said he was looking for Madam, Aunt, and Miss. When he saw you, he first paid his respects, then said that at dawn, he saw [Lianyi Zhou] leaving the north courtyard to say goodbye, carrying the lantern he had borrowed the night before, and hurriedly left Cuizhuang, apparently having already spoken to the master. [Uncle Fu] remembered the master came home drunk last night and didn’t dare disturb him.
After sunrise, not seeing the master come out, [Uncle Fu] knocked on the master bedroom door but got no response.
[Uncle Fu] said he tried to push the door, found it bolted from inside, and knowing the master always locked it before sleeping, knocked harder and called out, but still got no reply. He panicked when he couldn’t find Madam, Aunt, or Miss, and heard they had been invited to the pleasure boat yesterday. Only the Miss returned with the master at night, but wasn’t at Cuizhuang now.
[Uncle Fu] dared not act without orders and could only rush here to ask Madam, Aunt, and Miss to come back and decide what to do.
After hearing this, your aunt took you and [Chou Tian] home. [Chitong Zhou], [Sunian Yu], and [Lianyi Zhou] accompanied you, following [Uncle Fu] back to Cuizhuang. Entering the north courtyard, they knocked several times on the master bedroom door but still got no response.
Anxious, you told [Uncle Fu] to quickly get someone to break down the door. [Chitong Zhou], [Sunian Yu], and [Lianyi Zhou] didn’t wait for [Uncle Fu] to call the servants and together broke down the door. As the bolt broke and the door finally opened, you entered and saw your father lying on his side on the bed, his face still showing the flush of drunkenness, yet completely motionless. [Chitong Zhou] went over to check, hurriedly told everyone that [Shouheng Cui] was already dead, and his body was cold.
Seeing your father dead, you burst into tears. [Chou Tian], just as she did when Madam died, held you in her arms again. The small window was still open from the inside, the brazier was overturned by the wall, and there was an empty bowl on the table by the bed.
